# Elixir Ecto Sandbox Testing — Domain Reference

> The canonical reference for the `elixir-ecto-sandbox-test-composite` skill. Read this when you need to understand *why* a sandbox test is failing, not just *how* to patch the symptom. Everything else in this skill — the scripts, the cheatsheet, the anti-patterns list — is a shortcut that only makes sense if you hold the mental model in this document.

## 1. Introduction — Why Ecto Sandbox Testing Is Hard

Ecto's `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox` is one of the most elegant pieces of infrastructure in the Elixir ecosystem and one of the most commonly misused. The elegance: every test runs inside its own database transaction, which is rolled back on exit, so tests never pollute each other's state and you can run hundreds of them concurrently. The misuse: the instant a test involves more than one process — a `Task`, a `GenServer`, a LiveView mount, an Oban worker, a Phoenix Channel — the sandbox's invisible contract kicks in, and if you break it, your tests raise `DBConnection.OwnershipError` in ways that look like "flaky CI" but are actually deterministic bugs wearing a disguise.

The reason this skill exists at all is that a real Elixir+Phoenix application at BoothIQ.com — real production code, real test suite, real CI — had an LLM agent step into a debugging session and produce the following recommendations while the tests were red:

1. "Let me add some seed data to the test database so the query returns something." *(The sandbox transaction was rolled back before the assertion ever ran; the agent was looking at dev DB state from a Tidewave MCP tool and thought it was the test DB.)*
2. "Set `async: false` on this test — it's flaky because of concurrency." *(The actual bug was a missing `allow/3` call after `Task.async`. Disabling async hid the symptom and doubled the suite runtime.)*
3. "The `Repo.all/1` call is returning an empty list. Have you checked that the migration ran?" *(The migration had run; the sandbox had checked out a connection for pid A and the query was running from pid B.)*
4. *(Most damning)* "I don't see an `allow/3` call anywhere in your test file — I don't think that function exists in `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox`." *(It has existed since Ecto 2.0.)*

That session is the reason for this skill. We call it "the BoothIQ post-mortem" and it is the ghost that haunts every section below. If you are tempted to recommend `async: false`, stop and ask yourself whether the current model would have said the same thing. If the answer is yes, you are about to make the same mistake.

The broader stakes: a test suite that fails intermittently is worse than no test suite at all. Developers stop trusting it, CI becomes a slot machine, and "retry until green" becomes the debugging strategy. The exact failure modes sandbox testing produces — `OwnershipError` sometimes, silent empty results other times, state leaking from one test to the next — are the archetype of "flaky tests" that destroy confidence. Fixing them requires understanding the sandbox as a state machine, not a set of incantations.

This document teaches the state machine. It is opinionated. It will tell you that `async: false` is almost always wrong, that `SQL.Sandbox.mode(Repo, :shared)` is a confession of defeat, and that a LiveView test without `render_async/2` is a lie. These opinions are earned.

## 2. The Connection Ownership State Machine

Everything in sandbox testing makes sense once you hold this model in your head: **a sandbox transaction is owned by exactly one pid at a time, and every database query must originate from a pid the owner has authorized.**

Concretely, `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox` replaces the normal DBConnection pool with an ownership pool. When a process calls `checkout(Repo)`, the pool wraps that process's future queries in a transaction and *binds the transaction to the process's pid*. That process is now the *owner*. Every query the owner makes goes through the owner's transaction. When the owner exits (or calls `checkin`), the transaction rolls back.

So far, so simple. The complication is that most real Elixir code doesn't run in a single process. It spawns `Task`s, it talks to supervised GenServers, it goes through Phoenix's request pipeline which mounts LiveViews in their own processes, it enqueues Oban jobs that run in worker processes. Each of those processes has its own pid. By default, none of them can see the owner's sandbox transaction. If you call `Repo.insert!/1` from a `Task.async` child, the child pid will look up its own connection in the pool, find nothing, and raise:

```
** (DBConnection.OwnershipError) cannot find ownership process for #PID<0.456.0>.

When using ownership, you must manage connections in one of the four ways:
  ...
```

The error message is honest but not actionable. What it is really saying is: *your test's owner pid does not authorize this pid, so this pid has no database connection*. To fix it, you need one of two things:

1. **Make the other pid part of the owner's allow-list.** Call `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.allow(Repo, owner_pid, other_pid)` from any process that already has access. From that moment on, queries from `other_pid` route through `owner_pid`'s transaction.
2. **Transfer ownership to a pid with a longer lifetime.** Call `Sandbox.start_owner!(Repo, shared: false)` early in the test. This creates a *detached owner* whose only job is to hold the checkout. Then `allow/3` other pids against the detached owner rather than `self()`. This matters when children outlive the test pid (see §8).

Every pid in the process tree is in exactly one of four states at any moment:

| State | Meaning | Can query? |
|---|---|---|
| **Owner** | Called `checkout/2` (or `start_owner!/2` returned its pid) | Yes |
| **Allowed** | Owner called `allow/3` with this pid | Yes, via owner's transaction |
| **Unowned** | Neither of the above | No — `OwnershipError` |
| **Shared-victim** | Some process set `mode(Repo, :shared)` | Yes, but see §10 |

State transitions:
- **Unowned → Owner**: `checkout/2` or `start_owner!/2`.
- **Owner → Released**: owner exits, or `checkin/1`, or `stop_owner/1` on a detached owner. Transaction rolls back.
- **Unowned → Allowed**: `allow(Repo, owner, other_pid)` called from owner or another already-allowed pid.
- **Allowed → Released**: owner exits, OR the allowed pid itself exits.

The race condition everyone hits once: if you call `Task.async(fn -> Repo.insert!(...) end)` *and then* call `allow/3`, the task may have already tried (and failed) before the `allow` lands. The rule is: **allow first, then spawn the work that will use the connection**. For `Task.async` specifically this means grabbing the owner pid first:

```elixir
# WRONG — race
task = Task.async(fn -> Repo.insert!(%User{name: "Alice"}) end)
Sandbox.allow(Repo, self(), task.pid)
Task.await(task)

# RIGHT — the task captures `parent` by closure and calls allow from inside
parent = self()
task = Task.async(fn ->
  Sandbox.allow(Repo, parent, self())
  Repo.insert!(%User{name: "Alice"})
end)
Task.await(task)
```

The "correct" version has the child re-allow itself using the parent as the reference pid. This works because `allow/3` can be called by any pid that already has authorization — the parent does, so it's effectively delegating its access. The child doesn't race because it runs the `allow` synchronously before its first query.

Internalize this state machine. Every technique in the rest of this document is a variant of "get pid X into the Owner or Allowed state before it issues a query".

## 3. Setup: DataCase, start_owner!, and the async Tag

Every Phoenix project generated by `mix phx.new --ecto` ships a file at `test/support/data_case.ex` that looks roughly like this out of the box:

```elixir
defmodule MyApp.DataCase do
  use ExUnit.CaseTemplate

  using do
    quote do
      alias MyApp.Repo
      import Ecto
      import Ecto.Changeset
      import Ecto.Query
      import MyApp.DataCase
    end
  end

  setup tags do
    MyApp.DataCase.setup_sandbox(tags)
    :ok
  end

  def setup_sandbox(tags) do
    pid = Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.start_owner!(MyApp.Repo, shared: not tags[:async])
    on_exit(fn -> Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.stop_owner(pid) end)
  end
end
```

This template is almost right. There is one bug in it that has been copy-pasted into a thousand Elixir projects, and it's the reason so many teams think async tests "are flaky": `tags[:async]`.

In ExUnit, the `async` tag is not set on a test unless the test file explicitly declares `async: true`. When it's unset, `tags[:async]` returns `nil`. The sandbox setup then computes `shared: not nil`, which evaluates to `shared: true`, meaning **the sandbox enters shared mode for every test that forgot to declare `async: false` explicitly**. This is the opposite of what you want. Shared mode is a global switch that serializes all tests in the entire OS process against a single connection. In a freshly-generated Phoenix app with three tests, you won't notice. In a 2000-test suite, it tanks parallelism and causes "mystery" cross-test pollution because one test's leftover row survives into the next.

The fix is to be explicit about the default:

```elixir
def setup_sandbox(tags) do
  pid = Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.start_owner!(
    MyApp.Repo,
    shared: not Map.get(tags, :async, false)
  )
  on_exit(fn -> Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.stop_owner(pid) end)
end
```

`Map.get(tags, :async, false)` treats a missing `async` tag as `async: false` explicitly, so a test that didn't declare async stays in shared mode intentionally, but a test that did declare `async: true` gets `shared: false` — which is what unblocks parallel execution. This is a one-character fix with a 10x consequence on a large suite.

Why `start_owner!/2` and not the older `checkout/2`? Two reasons:

1. **Detached owner lifetime.** `checkout(Repo)` binds the transaction to the calling pid, which is the test pid. If your test spawns children via `start_supervised` or `live_isolated`, and those children race with the test pid's shutdown, you get a torn-down connection mid-query. `start_owner!(Repo, shared: false)` creates a *separate* pid whose only job is to hold the checkout. The test pid can exit without killing the transaction; the transaction rolls back only when the test explicitly calls `stop_owner/1` in `on_exit`.

2. **Idempotent teardown.** `stop_owner/1` is safe to call twice and safe to call against an already-dead owner. `checkin/1` is not. On_exit callbacks are called in LIFO order and sometimes a test's own cleanup kills the owner first — `stop_owner/1` tolerates this.

The pattern to memorize:

```elixir
defmodule MyApp.DataCase do
  use ExUnit.CaseTemplate

  using do
    quote do
      alias MyApp.Repo
      import Ecto.Query
      import MyApp.DataCase
    end
  end

  setup tags do
    pid =
      Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.start_owner!(
        MyApp.Repo,
        shared: not Map.get(tags, :async, false)
      )

    on_exit(fn -> Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.stop_owner(pid) end)

    {:ok, owner: pid}
  end
end
```

Exposing `owner: pid` in the context is not decoration — it's a gift to the test body, which will need the detached owner's pid when it calls `allow/3` for spawned children. Don't call `self()` at that point; call `context.owner`. See §8 for why.

## 4. Spawn Sites and allow/3 — A Catalog

Every Elixir/Phoenix primitive that creates a new pid is a potential ownership violation. Here is the catalog. For each, we give the right place to call `allow/3` and the failure mode if you put it elsewhere.

### Task.async / Task.await

```elixir
parent = self()
task = Task.async(fn ->
  Sandbox.allow(Repo, parent, self())
  Repo.insert!(%Log{msg: "hello"})
end)
Task.await(task)
```

The `allow` goes inside the task body and captures `parent` by closure. Do not call `allow` on `task.pid` from the parent *after* the `Task.async` returns — the task may already have issued its first query before your `allow` lands.

### Task.start_link (fire-and-forget)

Same pattern. If you're not awaiting, you still need to authorize the child:

```elixir
parent = self()
Task.start_link(fn ->
  Sandbox.allow(Repo, parent, self())
  Repo.insert!(%Audit{})
end)
```

But this is a smell: a fire-and-forget task in a test means you're testing a side effect with no synchronization. Either `Task.async` + `await`, or use `Task.Supervisor.async_nolink` with explicit reaping.

### Task.Supervisor.async_nolink / async_stream

`Task.Supervisor` spawns tasks under a named supervisor, which means the task pids are *not* linked to the test pid. The allow pattern is the same (from inside the task body), but the teardown story is different: if the test exits before the task finishes, you get an orphaned process trying to query a dead transaction. Mitigation: use `async_stream_nolink` with a bounded timeout and always drain the stream before the test returns.

### spawn / spawn_link

Avoid in tests. If you must, treat them exactly like `Task.start_link`: first line of the spawned fun is `Sandbox.allow(Repo, parent, self())`.

### DynamicSupervisor.start_child

The child is started by the DynamicSupervisor's pid, not yours. The DynamicSupervisor itself is usually not part of the sandbox — it was started at application boot. You have two options:

1. **Allow the child after it starts**: `{:ok, pid} = DynamicSupervisor.start_child(sup, child_spec)` then `Sandbox.allow(Repo, self(), pid)` from the test. This races: the child may query before the `allow` lands.
2. **Initialize a "sandbox-aware" child** that calls `allow` in its `init/1` using a parent pid passed in the start args:

```elixir
def init({parent, state}) do
  Sandbox.allow(Repo, parent, self())
  {:ok, state}
end
```

### GenServer.start_link under a supervision tree

Same trap as DynamicSupervisor: the supervisor owns the start, and the GenServer is a stranger to the sandbox. If the GenServer is started via `start_supervised/1` in the test, use the detached-owner pattern from §8 — call `allow(Repo, context.owner, gs_pid)` with the detached owner, not `self()`.

### Phoenix.LiveView mount

LiveView runs in a separate process from the test pid and from the connection pid. The correct technique is the `Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox` plug, which reads a metadata header from the test's connection and calls `allow/3` on the LiveView's mount pid automatically. See §5 for the full wiring.

### Phoenix.Channel join

Channels also run in a separate process. The test pid joins the channel via `subscribe_and_join/3`, gets back a `socket` struct, and must then call `Sandbox.allow(Repo, self(), socket.channel_pid)` *before* sending any message that will trigger a DB query. See §6.

### Oban perform_job (inline mode)

If Oban is in `:inline` testing mode, jobs run in the caller's pid — no allow needed. If Oban is in `:manual` mode (the default), `Oban.drain_queue/1` runs jobs in worker pids that are not under your control. The test-friendly escape hatch is `Oban.Testing.perform_job/3`, which runs the worker's `perform/1` in the caller's pid, skipping the worker entirely. See §7.

### Presence tracker

`Phoenix.Presence` runs tracking in its own supervised process. `Process.whereis(MyAppWeb.Presence)` gives you its pid; allow it with `Sandbox.allow(Repo, self(), Process.whereis(MyAppWeb.Presence))` *once per test* that depends on Presence state.

### The race-condition rule, restated

For every spawn-site in this catalog, the race condition is identical: **the child may issue its first query before the parent's `allow/3` call lands**. Mitigations, in order of preference:

1. Have the child call `allow` on itself using a captured parent pid (works for closure-based spawns like `Task.async`).
2. Have the child call `allow` in its `init/1` callback using a pid from start args (works for supervised GenServers).
3. Use a framework-provided plug like `Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox` that injects the allow automatically (works for LiveView/Channel).
4. Only as a last resort: synchronize the spawn with a `Process.sleep/1` or a message handshake. Never commit this pattern.

## 5. LiveView Integration

A LiveView test is a process tree. Holding the shape in your head is 80% of the battle.

```
test_pid (ExUnit process, owns the sandbox transaction via detached owner)
 └─ connection_pid (Plug.Conn handling — short-lived)
     └─ socket_pid (LiveView process, started by Phoenix.LiveView.Channel)
         ├─ render_async task pids (one per assign_async call)
         ├─ handle_info tasks (Task.async spawned from handle_event)
         └─ Presence tracker pid (if used)
```

Every one of those pids needs to be in the "allowed" state. Hand-crafting all the allows for every test is insane, so Phoenix ships the `Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox` plug, which automates it for the connection layer. The plumbing looks like this.

**In `config/test.exs`:**

```elixir
config :my_app, sql_sandbox: true
```

**In `lib/my_app_web/endpoint.ex`:**

```elixir
if Application.compile_env(:my_app, :sql_sandbox, false) do
  plug Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox,
    at: "/sandbox",
    repo: MyApp.Repo,
    timeout: 60_000
end
```

The `compile_env` guard is load-bearing: the plug must only be compiled into the endpoint in test. In production, a `/sandbox` route that accepts ownership metadata is a security hole. `compile_env` at compile time bakes the decision in; `Application.get_env` at runtime would leave a live attack surface.

**In `test/support/conn_case.ex`, wire the metadata header:**

```elixir
setup tags do
  pid =
    Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.start_owner!(
      MyApp.Repo,
      shared: not Map.get(tags, :async, false)
    )

  on_exit(fn -> Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.stop_owner(pid) end)

  conn =
    Phoenix.ConnTest.build_conn()
    |> Plug.Conn.put_req_header(
      "user-agent",
      Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox.metadata_for(MyApp.Repo, pid)
    )

  {:ok, conn: conn, owner: pid}
end
```

The `user-agent` header is not a typo. The plug reads sandbox metadata from a user-agent-shaped header specifically because it's one of the few headers that survives Phoenix's socket connect negotiation. You can configure a different header via the plug options, but the default is user-agent and going against it means fighting Phoenix for no reason.

**In the LiveView test body, connect with `:user_agent` in `connect_info`:**

```elixir
{:ok, view, html} =
  conn
  |> put_connect_info(%{user_agent: get_req_header(conn, "user-agent") |> List.first()})
  |> live("/dashboard")
```

Forgetting `put_connect_info` is the #1 reason a LiveView test suddenly can't see the DB — the metadata was in the HTTP header but didn't make it through the socket upgrade.

### render_async / assign_async

`assign_async/3` runs its callback in a supervised `Task`, which is a separate pid from the LiveView process. The `Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox` plug doesn't automatically allow those task pids; LiveView's own infrastructure does, because `assign_async` uses the same sandbox metadata the LiveView was mounted with. *But* your test won't see the result until you synchronize. That's what `render_async/2` is for:

```elixir
test "dashboard loads user metrics", %{conn: conn} do
  {:ok, view, _html} = live(conn, "/dashboard")

  # This will NOT see the async-loaded metrics:
  refute render(view) =~ "Total Users: 42"

  # This WILL:
  html = render_async(view, 1000)
  assert html =~ "Total Users: 42"
end
```

`render_async/2` blocks until all pending async assigns have resolved. The timeout defaults to 100ms which is too short for anything touching the DB; always pass an explicit timeout of 500ms–2s. If your test passes without `render_async` and asserts on the initial render, you're probably asserting on the *loading* state, not the final one, and you'll discover this the day the async callback gets slower.

### Compile-time guard

We already mentioned `Application.compile_env`, but it's worth its own paragraph. The pattern is:

```elixir
if Application.compile_env(:my_app, :sql_sandbox, false) do
  plug Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox, at: "/sandbox", repo: MyApp.Repo
end
```

This is *compile-time* branching. The plug code is only emitted into the compiled BEAM file when `:sql_sandbox` is `true`, which is only true in `config/test.exs`. In production builds, the plug doesn't exist at all — there is no `/sandbox` endpoint to hit, no metadata header to sniff, no way for an external client to call `allow/3` on a production Repo. If you instead wrote `if Application.get_env(...)`, the plug *would* be compiled in and only gated at runtime, which is both less efficient and less secure.

### Common LiveView failure modes

- **Assertions on loading state**: you rendered before `render_async`, saw the spinner, and asserted on that. Fix: add `render_async(view, 1000)`.
- **"No sandbox metadata in connection"**: you forgot `put_connect_info`. Fix: propagate the user-agent header into `connect_info`.
- **`OwnershipError` from inside `handle_event`**: the event spawned a `Task.async` that didn't allow itself. Fix: move the DB work back into the LiveView process, OR have the task allow using the LiveView's pid.
- **Test passes locally, fails in CI**: the assign_async task is slower in CI and `render_async`'s timeout is too short. Fix: bump the timeout to 2000ms.
- **LiveView crashes with `DBConnection.OwnershipError` on reconnect**: you started the test with `checkout/2` instead of `start_owner!/2`, the test pid exited after the initial assertion, and the LiveView reconnect raced the transaction teardown. Fix: detached owner.

## 6. Channels Integration

A Phoenix Channel test starts with `subscribe_and_join/3`:

```elixir
{:ok, _payload, socket} =
  MyAppWeb.UserSocket
  |> socket("user_id", %{user_id: user.id})
  |> subscribe_and_join(MyAppWeb.RoomChannel, "room:lobby")
```

`socket.channel_pid` is the pid of the channel process. It's spawned by `Phoenix.Channel.Server` and is not part of the test's sandbox by default. Before you push any event that will trigger a DB query, you must authorize it:

```elixir
Sandbox.allow(Repo, self(), socket.channel_pid)
```

This gets tedious when you're joining multiple channels in one test, which is the shape of most realistic Channel tests — "a user is in the lobby, joins a game, the game broadcasts a move". The idiomatic pattern is a helper in `ChannelCase`:

```elixir
defmodule MyAppWeb.ChannelCase do
  use ExUnit.CaseTemplate

  # ...

  def join_and_allow(socket, channel, topic, payload \\ %{}) do
    {:ok, reply, socket} = subscribe_and_join(socket, channel, topic, payload)
    Sandbox.allow(Repo, self(), socket.channel_pid)
    {:ok, reply, socket}
  end
end
```

Using it:

```elixir
{:ok, _, lobby_socket} = join_and_allow(socket, MyAppWeb.LobbyChannel, "lobby")
{:ok, _, game_socket} = join_and_allow(socket, MyAppWeb.GameChannel, "game:42")
```

### The :already_shared dedup problem

`Sandbox.allow/3` is idempotent *but* if you mistakenly switch to shared mode and then try to call `allow` from a pid that's already seeing shared state, you can get `{:already, :shared}` errors. The defense is a per-test `MapSet` of pids you've already allowed:

```elixir
setup do
  {:ok, allowed: MapSet.new()}
end

defp allow_once(pid, context) do
  if MapSet.member?(context.allowed, pid) do
    context
  else
    Sandbox.allow(Repo, self(), pid)
    %{context | allowed: MapSet.put(context.allowed, pid)}
  end
end
```

In practice, most suites don't need this — the helper approach above is enough — but if you're stitching together a deeply-nested channel test, the dedup set prevents mysterious failures.

### Presence

`Phoenix.Presence` runs its tracker in a separate supervised process. If your test asserts on presence state, you need to allow the tracker:

```elixir
Sandbox.allow(Repo, self(), Process.whereis(MyAppWeb.Presence))
```

Do this once in `setup` for any test file that touches Presence. `Process.whereis/1` returns `nil` if the Presence tracker isn't running, which is a red flag that your app's supervision tree isn't booting in test — check `test_helper.exs` for a missing `start_supervised` or an overly aggressive `:manual` startup config.

### Multi-shard and read-replica handling

If you run separate Repos for read-only replicas (e.g., `MyApp.Repo.Primary`, `MyApp.Repo.Replica`), every allow call needs to happen on every Repo. A helper:

```elixir
defp allow_all_repos(pid) do
  Enum.each([MyApp.Repo.Primary, MyApp.Repo.Replica], fn repo ->
    Sandbox.allow(repo, self(), pid)
  end)
end
```

Run this in your channel join helper. Forgetting one Repo will produce "OwnershipError: cannot find ownership process" *only on queries that happen to use the read replica* — classic flake pattern.

### The CI-slower-than-local gotcha

Channels are slightly slower to join in CI than locally because CI machines have more contention. If your test uses a 100ms timeout on `assert_push/3` or similar, it will pass locally and fail in CI. Default to 500ms or 1s in channel tests, and prefer `assert_receive/3` with a generous timeout over `assert_push/3` with a short one.

## 7. Oban Integration

Oban is the second-most-common source of sandbox ownership bugs, after LiveView. The root cause is the same as everywhere else: Oban runs jobs in worker pids that the test does not directly spawn, and those pids are not part of the sandbox.

### The three testing modes

Oban has three testing modes, configured via `config :my_app, Oban, testing: MODE`:

- **`:inline`** — jobs run immediately, in the caller's pid, during `Oban.insert/1`. No allow needed because the caller is the test pid. Simple, fast, good for contract tests. Downside: you're not actually testing the queue mechanism.
- **`:manual`** (default) — `Oban.insert/1` enqueues a job but does not run it. You run jobs manually via `Oban.drain_queue/1` or `perform_job/3`. This is what you want for most tests.
- **`:shared`** — jobs run in real worker processes but share the test's sandbox connection. Rarely the right choice; use the cost ledger in §10 before committing.

### The commit-visibility trap

Here is the bug that catches everyone once. In `:manual` mode:

```elixir
test "job processes user signup" do
  user = Repo.insert!(%User{name: "Alice"})
  {:ok, _job} = Oban.insert(MyApp.Workers.Welcome.new(%{user_id: user.id}))

  # This finds zero jobs:
  assert [] = Oban.drain_queue(queue: :default)
end
```

Wait, what? We just inserted the job. Why does `drain_queue` find nothing?

Because `drain_queue` runs in a *worker pid*, and that worker pid is not in the sandbox's allow-list, so when the worker queries the `oban_jobs` table, it sees nothing — the insert happened inside the test's uncommitted transaction, which is invisible to the worker pid. The "zero jobs" result is not Oban being broken; it is the sandbox correctly isolating transactions.

Three fixes, in order of preference:

1. **Use `perform_job/3` to sidestep the worker pid entirely.** This runs `MyApp.Workers.Welcome.perform/1` directly in the test pid, which already has transaction access:

   ```elixir
   assert :ok = perform_job(MyApp.Workers.Welcome, %{user_id: user.id})
   ```

   The args must be string-keyed, not atom-keyed, because `perform_job` round-trips through JSONB serialization to mirror what Oban does in production.

2. **Use `assert_enqueued/1` for contract tests.** If you only care that the job was enqueued with the right args, not that it executed, `assert_enqueued/1` runs in the test pid and checks the sandbox transaction directly:

   ```elixir
   assert_enqueued worker: MyApp.Workers.Welcome, args: %{"user_id" => user.id}
   ```

3. **Allow the worker pid before draining.** This only works if you flip to `:shared` mode for the test (see §10). Not recommended as the default.

### perform_job/3 in detail

`Oban.Testing.perform_job/3` is the single most useful function in the Oban test surface. It:

1. Builds a `%Oban.Job{}` with your worker, args, and (optionally) metadata.
2. JSON-encodes and decodes the args to mirror Postgres JSONB serialization, so atom keys become string keys and integers survive as integers but structs don't.
3. Calls `YourWorker.perform(job)` directly.
4. Returns the worker's result (`:ok`, `{:ok, value}`, `{:error, reason}`, `{:snooze, seconds}`, `{:discard, reason}`).

Because it runs in the test pid, all DB queries go through the test's sandbox transaction. You can assert on side effects:

```elixir
test "welcome worker sends email" do
  user = Repo.insert!(%User{name: "Alice", email: "alice@example.com"})
  assert :ok = perform_job(MyApp.Workers.Welcome, %{"user_id" => user.id})
  assert_receive {:email_sent, "alice@example.com"}
end
```

Note `"user_id"` (string), not `:user_id` (atom). Using atoms here will silently pass in `perform_job` but fail in production when the worker picks up a real JSONB-decoded job and gets string keys.

### with_testing_mode for per-block overrides

If a single test needs a different mode than the rest of the file:

```elixir
test "integration: full queue drain" do
  Oban.Testing.with_testing_mode(:inline, fn ->
    Oban.insert!(MyApp.Workers.Welcome.new(%{"user_id" => user.id}))
    # job ran synchronously, side effects are visible
  end)
end
```

`with_testing_mode/2` flips the mode for the duration of the callback and restores it after. Use this sparingly — it's a signal that the test is checking something `perform_job` could check more cleanly.

### assert_enqueued for pure contract tests

The most test-robust Oban assertion is the one that doesn't run anything:

```elixir
test "signup flow enqueues welcome job" do
  {:ok, user} = Accounts.create_user(%{name: "Alice", email: "alice@example.com"})

  assert_enqueued worker: MyApp.Workers.Welcome,
                  args: %{"user_id" => user.id},
                  queue: :default
end
```

This asserts "the job was put in the queue with these args" without caring whether it ever ran. It's the right assertion when you're testing the *enqueuer* (the business logic that decided to schedule a job) not the *worker* (the logic that executes it). Testing those separately is a win for maintainability — the worker gets its own test file with `perform_job` calls, and the enqueuer's tests don't care about the worker's implementation.

## 8. Connection Ownership Transfer — The Detached Owner

Everything in §3–§7 assumes the test pid is the owner. Sometimes it can't be. The clearest case: you call `start_supervised/1` to start a GenServer for the test, and the GenServer lives longer than the test pid's ExUnit cleanup. Or you use `live_isolated/3` to mount a LiveView whose process tree outlives the test. Or you spawn an `async_nolink` task that the test doesn't wait on.

In all of these cases, the test pid's death triggers transaction teardown *before* the child finishes, which raises `DBConnection.OwnershipError` in the child process. The fix is to *detach the ownership from the test pid*:

```elixir
setup tags do
  # detached_owner is a separate pid whose ONLY job is to hold the checkout
  detached_owner =
    Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.start_owner!(
      MyApp.Repo,
      shared: not Map.get(tags, :async, false)
    )

  on_exit(fn -> Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.stop_owner(detached_owner) end)

  {:ok, owner: detached_owner}
end
```

`start_owner!/2` returns a pid. That pid is a supervised process whose mailbox is empty and whose only responsibility is to survive as long as the test needs a sandbox transaction. It's not tied to `self()`. When the test pid exits, the detached owner is still alive, so the transaction is still alive, so child processes can still query.

The `on_exit` callback is responsible for tearing the detached owner down after all other cleanup has run. ExUnit runs `on_exit` callbacks in LIFO order, so the `stop_owner` call happens *after* any `start_supervised` children have been terminated. This ordering matters: if you stopped the owner first, the children would see a torn-down transaction during their shutdown handlers.

When a test spawns a child, it calls `allow/3` against the *detached owner*, not `self()`:

```elixir
test "background worker processes events", %{owner: owner} do
  {:ok, worker} = start_supervised({MyApp.EventWorker, parent: owner})
  # inside EventWorker.init/1:
  #   Sandbox.allow(Repo, parent, self())
  send(worker, {:event, %{type: "signup"}})
  assert_receive {:processed, _}
end
```

The `EventWorker.init/1` callback calls `Sandbox.allow(Repo, owner, self())`. Since `owner` is the detached pid and not the test pid, and since the detached pid is still alive, the allow succeeds. After the test body finishes, ExUnit calls `on_exit` callbacks; `start_supervised` tears down the EventWorker first, then `stop_owner(owner)` closes the transaction.

This pattern is the difference between "works 95% of the time" and "works 100% of the time" for any test with a background process. It's the default in our DataCase template and should be the default in yours. The performance cost is zero — `start_owner!` just spawns one extra pid per test, which is negligible compared to the query overhead.

## 9. The Async Justification Ladder

`async: true` is not a feature. It is the default. A test that declares `async: false` is asking the suite to run slower and is claiming that the test cannot be safely parallelized. That claim needs justification.

Here is the justification ladder, from most acceptable to never-acceptable:

**Acceptable reasons for `async: false`:**

1. **The test calls `Application.put_env/3` or `Application.delete_env/2`.** Application env is process-global within a node. Two tests that mutate the same key in parallel will interfere. This is a legitimate reason.
2. **The test uses `Mox` with `:global` mode.** Mox in global mode installs expectations on the named process, which is shared across the node. Use `:private` mode instead if possible, but if the code under test doesn't accept a parent pid for mock lookup, `:global` is unavoidable and justifies `async: false`.
3. **The test touches a named GenServer that doesn't support concurrent access.** If the production design uses a singleton for a good reason (e.g., rate limiter, in-memory cache), and the test has to interact with that singleton, you can't parallelize.
4. **The test hits a non-sandboxed external resource** (file system, ports, a named ETS table). Sandbox doesn't cover these.
5. **The test uses `:meck` or similar global-patching mocks.** These replace module code globally. `async: false` is mandatory.

**Never-acceptable reasons:**

1. **"The test was flaky."** A flaky test is a bug, not a tag. Fix the bug (see §12).
2. **"It touches the database."** This is what the sandbox is for. If it's flaky in the database, you have a missing allow, a missing `render_async`, or a missing detached owner. Fix that.
3. **"Other tests in the file are `async: false`."** Cargo cult. Each test's async setting should stand on its own merits.
4. **"I don't understand why it's failing, but turning off async fixes it."** This is the BoothIQ post-mortem pattern. You are turning a deterministic bug into a slower deterministic bug. Dig in.
5. **"The reviewer said to."** Show them this section.

Enforcement: every `async: false` declaration in the test suite should be accompanied by a comment explaining *which* justification it's using. Without the comment, assume it's an unjustified regression and challenge it in review. Aim for a grep-able convention:

```elixir
use MyApp.DataCase, async: false  # async: false because Mox :global mode (#3)
```

A quick sweep of your test suite for `async: false` without an explanatory comment is a high-ROI refactor.

## 10. The Shared-Mode Cost Ledger

`Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.mode(Repo, :shared)` is the nuclear option. It flips the entire sandbox into a mode where every process in the node — not just the ones you allow — sees the same transaction. It's tempting because it makes ownership errors disappear, and it's the wrong choice 95% of the time.

Three costs you pay for shared mode:

1. **Loss of async.** Shared mode serializes all tests using the Repo against a single connection. Your `async: true` tests will run, but they'll block on the shared lock, effectively turning the entire test file into `async: false`. On a 2000-test suite this matters.
2. **Cross-test pollution.** In shared mode, a test that leaks state (an unrolled-back `INSERT`, an unexpected `TRUNCATE`) will leak into the next test. Sandbox's whole pitch is "every test rolls back"; shared mode weakens that guarantee because multiple pids are sharing the same transaction and the cleanup is sometimes ambiguous.
3. **Debugging tax.** When a shared-mode test fails, you can't easily tell *which* test left behind the state the current test is complaining about. Bisection still works but is slower because the dependency is at the file level, not the test level.

If you are going to use shared mode, write a comment justifying it:

```elixir
setup do
  Sandbox.mode(MyApp.Repo, :shared)
  # Shared mode: the app under test uses a GenServer pool that doesn't
  # accept a parent pid for allow propagation, and we've chosen not to
  # refactor production code for a single integration test file.
  :ok
end
```

The alternatives ladder, in order, before reaching for shared mode:

1. **Use `allow/3` explicitly on every spawn site.** Tedious but correct. See §4.
2. **Use `start_supervised` with caller tracking.** Have the supervised child capture `self()` at start time and call `allow` in its `init/1`.
3. **Use telemetry events to observe behavior** instead of querying the DB from a worker. This is especially useful for Oban tests — `:telemetry.attach` to the right event, assert on it, skip the worker's own DB work.
4. **Use `:inline` testing mode** (for Oban) or the equivalent synchronous dispatcher for your background system.
5. **Refactor production code** to accept a parent pid for sandbox propagation. This is a legitimate refactor: "the production code should not know about the sandbox" is only defensible until you've tried every other option.
6. **Shared mode.** If you're here, you've lost the game but at least you know you've lost it.

## 11. The Tidewave Dev-vs-Test Trap

Tidewave is an excellent tool. It's an MCP server that exposes your *development* database to Claude as a queryable resource. In a normal coding session, Claude can ask "what's in the users table" and get an answer without you having to paste a schema. It's the kind of tool that makes the IDE feel telepathic.

During test debugging, Tidewave is a trap.

Here is the exact mistake, lifted from the BoothIQ post-mortem:

> **Developer:** My test `test "returns recent bookings"` is failing. The assertion is `assert length(bookings) == 3` but `length(bookings)` is 0.
>
> **Claude (using Tidewave):** Let me check what's in the bookings table. *(calls Tidewave query tool)* I see 47 bookings in the bookings table, including three from the last week. The query should be returning results. Have you checked that your `Repo.all/1` call includes the right `where` clause? Let me also add some seed data to make sure...

Three things are wrong with this response:

1. **Claude is looking at the dev database.** The test sees the *test* database, and inside the test's sandbox transaction the bookings table is empty (or contains only what the test inserted). Tidewave sees the dev database, which has 47 bookings from development use. These are two completely different views of the world.
2. **The proposed fix ("add seed data") would poison the dev database**, not the test. Tidewave's write tools act on the dev database. Any `INSERT` Claude runs through Tidewave during a test debugging session will leave debris in dev and do nothing useful for the test.
3. **Claude doesn't know it's wrong.** The `length(bookings) == 3` assertion wasn't contradicted by Tidewave's 47-row result — in fact, 47 > 3, which superficially *supports* the assertion, so Claude followed the wrong clue.

The fix is procedural. Every MCP tool call made during a test debugging session should go through the **MCP Audit Protocol**:

1. **Classify the failing process.** Is the process that's raising the error a test process (started by ExUnit, inside a sandbox transaction)? Or is it a dev/prod process (started by `iex -S mix phx.server`)? For test failures, the answer is always "test".
2. **Declare the tool's relevance.** For every MCP tool available, ask: does this tool see the test's view of the database? If it's Tidewave, the answer is no — Tidewave sees dev. The tool is **irrelevant** for this debugging session.
3. **Route through the test process.** The only source of truth during a test failure is what the test process itself sees. That means `IO.inspect/2` inside the test body, ExUnit's `--trace` flag, or temporarily adding a `Logger.error/2` call to the code under test. *Not* an MCP tool.
4. **Justify or abort.** If you still think an MCP tool is relevant, write a comment explaining why. If you can't, abort the tool call.

We mark every MCP tool call during test debugging with a `[mcp-audit]` comment so a human reviewer can verify:

```elixir
# [mcp-audit] skipping Tidewave query — test process sees test DB,
# Tidewave sees dev DB. Using IO.inspect instead.
IO.inspect(Repo.all(Booking), label: "bookings in test transaction")
```

Concrete example of Claude catching itself:

> **Developer:** The test `test "returns recent bookings"` is failing. `length(bookings)` is 0, but I know there are bookings.
>
> **Claude (with audit protocol):** Before checking anything, let me classify: the failing process is an ExUnit test, so it's inside a sandbox transaction. I have Tidewave available, but Tidewave reads the dev database, which is not what the test sees. I'm going to skip Tidewave and instead add `IO.inspect(Repo.all(Booking))` inside the test, which will print what the test process itself sees. *(makes the edit)* When you run it, the output will tell us whether the test's sandbox has the bookings inserted at all. If it's empty, we're looking at a setup bug; if it has rows but the query returns nothing, we're looking at a query bug.

The protocol sounds bureaucratic. It's not — it's a 15-second check that prevents a 2-hour debugging session in which Claude chases phantoms in the wrong database.

## 12. Flaky Test Diagnosis

"Passes alone, fails in suite" is not flakiness. It is a deterministic bug that depends on test ordering or inter-test state. Treat it as a bug.

### Step 1: bisect with captured seed

ExUnit supports `--seed N` for ordering. When a test fails in CI, the seed is in the output — capture it. Re-run locally with that seed: `mix test --seed 123456 --trace`. If the failure reproduces, you have determinism and you can bisect.

`mix test --seed 123456 --only failing_tag --include failing_tag` narrows to the failing test. Now start removing other tests from the suite to find which ones it depends on:

```
mix test --seed 123456 test/my_app/failing_test.exs test/my_app/other_test.exs
```

Incrementally add test files until you find the pair that fails together. This is slow but deterministic.

### Step 2: walk the hypothesis tree

Once you have a minimal failing pair, the bug is one of four things:

**(a) Ownership leak.** Test A spawned a process that outlived the test and queried the DB after the transaction rolled back. When test B starts, the leaked pid is still around and may interfere. Symptom: `OwnershipError` in test B, not in test A. Fix: detached owner + proper `on_exit` cleanup in test A.

**(b) Global state.** Test A set `Application.put_env/3`, wrote to a `:public` ETS table, or called `start_link` on a named process without cleaning up. Test B depends on the original state. Symptom: test B's assertion fails on data that "isn't there", but the DB is fine. Fix: audit test A for `Application.put_env`, ETS writes, and named process starts; clean them up in `on_exit`.

**(c) Name collision.** Two tests spawn a process with the same registered name. The second `start_link` fails with `{:error, {:already_started, pid}}`, which may be swallowed by `start_supervised` and produce a cryptic downstream failure. Symptom: works in isolation, fails when run with specific other tests. Fix: use via-tuples (`{:via, Registry, {MyApp.Registry, key}}`) or prefix names with `self()` hashes.

**(d) Non-ownership flakiness.** Time-dependent code, random ordering, external HTTP calls, filesystem races. Symptom: sometimes passes in CI, sometimes fails, no obvious dependency on other tests. Fix: mock the nondeterministic thing.

### Step 3: eliminate the leaf

For each hypothesis, there's a fast diagnostic:

- **(a) Ownership leak**: grep the failing test's setup for `start_supervised` or `Task.async` without `await`. Check `on_exit` for teardown. Add a `:telemetry` handler on `[:ecto, :repo, :query]` and print pids.
- **(b) Global state**: grep test A for `Application.put_env`, `:ets.new`, `Process.register`. Add cleanup.
- **(c) Name collision**: look for `start_link(name: MyApp.Foo)` or `Registry.register`. Switch to via-tuples.
- **(d) Nondeterminism**: add a seed to `:rand.seed/1` in setup. Mock HTTP. Use `System.monotonic_time/0` instead of `:erlang.system_time/0`.

### Step 4: prove the fix with three different seeds

Once you think you've fixed it, prove it:

```
mix test --seed 111 test/failing_test.exs test/other_test.exs
mix test --seed 222 test/failing_test.exs test/other_test.exs
mix test --seed 333 test/failing_test.exs test/other_test.exs
```

All three must pass. If any one fails, the fix is incomplete. If all three pass, commit with a message that mentions the seed:

```
fix: prevent ownership leak in EventWorker test
The test was spawning a supervised worker without a detached owner,
so the worker's cleanup would query the transaction post-rollback.
Reproduced with --seed 123456; fix verified with 111/222/333.
```

The commit message with three seeds is gold for the next person who sees a similar flake.

## 13. Anti-Patterns Catalog

A deduplicated list drawn from all 11 variant dimensions. Each entry is a mistake we've seen in real code, with the shortest possible explanation of why it's wrong.

1. **`async: false` as a fix for OwnershipError.** The bug is an unauthorized spawn, not concurrency. Fix the allow.
2. **`checkout/2` instead of `start_owner!/2` in DataCase.** Ties the transaction to the test pid's lifetime; breaks any test with a long-lived child.
3. **`tags[:async]` instead of `Map.get(tags, :async, false)`.** `tags[:async]` is `nil` when unset, `not nil` is `true`, so you enter shared mode by default.
4. **Allowing a `Task.async` pid from the parent *after* spawn.** Races with the child's first query.
5. **Forgetting `render_async/2` in a LiveView test with `assign_async`.** Asserts on loading state.
6. **Missing `put_connect_info` in a LiveView test.** Sandbox metadata header doesn't make it through the socket upgrade.
7. **Using `Application.get_env` instead of `Application.compile_env` for the sandbox plug guard.** Leaves a live `/sandbox` endpoint in production.
8. **Not allowing `socket.channel_pid` after `subscribe_and_join`.** Next channel message that touches the DB raises.
9. **Allowing channel pid once but joining two channels.** Only the first channel is authorized.
10. **Forgetting Presence tracker pid allow.** Presence-related queries raise.
11. **Forgetting read-replica Repo allow.** Read queries raise but only sometimes.
12. **`Oban.drain_queue/1` in `:manual` mode expecting to see uncommitted jobs.** Worker pid can't see the test's transaction.
13. **Atom-keyed args in `perform_job/3`.** Masks a real bug that will appear in production with JSONB-decoded string keys.
14. **Missing `Oban.Testing.with_testing_mode/2` wrapper for a test that needs `:inline`.** Falls back to `:manual` and the test silently does nothing.
15. **Using `assert_enqueued/1` *and* `perform_job/3` in the same test.** Double-testing the same thing; one or the other, not both.
16. **Shared mode declared without a justification comment.** Should be review-failing; without the comment you can't tell if it was intentional.
17. **Shared mode declared for a single failing test instead of fixing the underlying allow.** Turns one flaky test into a globally-slow file.
18. **Allowing from `self()` when using a detached owner.** The detached owner is the transaction holder; `self()` is a red herring.
19. **Calling `stop_owner/1` before `start_supervised` children have torn down.** Children race the transaction teardown.
20. **`start_supervised` without `allow`ing the supervised pid.** Classic ownership leak; worker raises when it tries to query.
21. **`Task.Supervisor.async_nolink` that the test doesn't drain.** Orphaned task queries torn-down transaction.
22. **Using Tidewave MCP queries to debug test failures.** Wrong database; the test never saw those rows.
23. **"Seed the test database to make the test pass".** The test's sandbox rolled back; no seed you add outside the transaction is visible inside it.
24. **Asserting on `Repo.all/1` from a spawned process without an allow.** Returns `[]`, not an error, if you happen to hit a Repo that's in a weird state.
25. **Starting a sandbox test with `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.checkout/2` and no `on_exit`.** Checkout persists across tests; next test fails mysteriously.
26. **Mutating `Application.put_env` without restoring it in `on_exit`.** Global state leak, hits the next test.
27. **Using a named GenServer in an `async: true` test without recognizing the collision.** Two parallel tests hit the same `start_link`.
28. **Calling `Sandbox.mode(Repo, :shared)` inside a test body instead of setup.** Mode flip mid-test corrupts state.
29. **Using `Process.sleep/1` to wait for a spawned process's DB write.** Should be a synchronization primitive (message, monitor, `assert_receive`).
30. **Depending on insertion order for `Repo.all/1` without an `order_by`.** Not a sandbox bug but commonly entangled with sandbox debugging because the symptom is "sometimes the test sees the rows in the wrong order".

## 14. Worked Examples

Four realistic test files, each exercising multiple variant dimensions. These are the shape real test files take when the domain is handled correctly.

### Example 1: Task.async + allow/3 + async: true

```elixir
defmodule MyApp.AuditLoggerTest do
  use MyApp.DataCase, async: true

  alias MyApp.{Repo, Audit, AuditLogger}

  test "logs asynchronously from parallel tasks", %{owner: owner} do
    parent = self()

    tasks =
      for i <- 1..10 do
        Task.async(fn ->
          # allow ourselves against the detached owner, not parent
          Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.allow(Repo, owner, self())
          AuditLogger.log("event_#{i}")
        end)
      end

    Enum.each(tasks, &Task.await/1)

    audit_count = Repo.aggregate(Audit, :count)
    assert audit_count == 10
  end
end
```

Notes: `async: true` is explicit. The owner pid comes from the DataCase context, not `self()`. Every task allows itself inside its own fun. The assertion runs in the test pid, which owns the transaction, so it sees the tasks' writes.

### Example 2: LiveView with detached owner + assign_async + render_async

```elixir
defmodule MyAppWeb.DashboardLiveTest do
  use MyAppWeb.ConnCase, async: true

  import Phoenix.LiveViewTest

  test "renders async-loaded metrics after connect", %{conn: conn, owner: _owner} do
    for i <- 1..5 do
      MyApp.Repo.insert!(%MyApp.User{name: "User #{i}"})
    end

    {:ok, view, html} = live(conn, "/dashboard")

    # Initial render: loading state
    assert html =~ "Loading metrics..."

    # Wait for assign_async tasks to finish
    html = render_async(view, 1000)
    assert html =~ "Total Users: 5"
  end
end
```

Notes: `ConnCase` sets up the `Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox` plug metadata in the user-agent header. `live/2` propagates it via `connect_info`. `render_async(view, 1000)` with an explicit 1000ms timeout is the difference between passing and flaking in CI.

### Example 3: Oban :manual + perform_job

```elixir
defmodule MyApp.Workers.WelcomeTest do
  use MyApp.DataCase, async: true

  alias MyApp.{Repo, User}
  alias MyApp.Workers.Welcome

  test "welcome worker marks user as welcomed" do
    user = Repo.insert!(%User{name: "Alice", email: "alice@example.com"})

    assert :ok = perform_job(Welcome, %{"user_id" => user.id})

    reloaded = Repo.get!(User, user.id)
    assert reloaded.welcomed_at != nil
  end

  test "welcome worker discards jobs for nonexistent users" do
    assert {:discard, :user_not_found} =
             perform_job(Welcome, %{"user_id" => 0})
  end
end
```

Notes: `"user_id"` is string-keyed. `perform_job` runs `Welcome.perform/1` directly in the test pid, so the `Repo.get!` the worker does uses the test's sandbox transaction. `async: true` is fine because the test doesn't flip Oban's testing mode.

### Example 4: ChannelCase helper with Presence

```elixir
defmodule MyAppWeb.GameChannelTest do
  use MyAppWeb.ChannelCase, async: true

  alias MyAppWeb.{UserSocket, GameChannel, Presence}

  setup %{owner: owner} = context do
    Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.allow(
      MyApp.Repo,
      owner,
      Process.whereis(Presence)
    )

    socket =
      socket(UserSocket, "user_1", %{user_id: 1})

    {:ok, _, socket} = join_and_allow(socket, GameChannel, "game:lobby")

    {:ok, Map.put(context, :socket, socket)}
  end

  test "broadcasts move to all players", %{socket: socket} do
    ref = push(socket, "move", %{"from" => "e2", "to" => "e4"})
    assert_reply ref, :ok
    assert_broadcast "move_made", %{"from" => "e2", "to" => "e4"}
  end

  test "updates presence on join", %{socket: _socket} do
    assert %{"user_1" => %{metas: [_]}} = Presence.list("game:lobby")
  end
end
```

Notes: The Presence tracker's pid is allowed in `setup` using the detached owner. `join_and_allow/3` is a `ChannelCase` helper that does both `subscribe_and_join` and the channel pid allow. `async: true` works because we've authorized every pid the test touches.

## 15. When Not to Use This Skill

This skill is about `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox`. It does not cover:

- **Browser-based E2E tests (Wallaby, Hound).** Those use `Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox` on the server side (same plug we use for LiveView), but the client-side concerns — browser timing, JavaScript state, headless Chrome flakiness — are their own domain. The sandbox side is similar; the browser side is not.
- **Production pool configuration.** `Ecto.Repo`'s `:pool`, `:pool_size`, `:queue_target`, and `:queue_interval` options are for production tuning. The `Sandbox` pool replaces them in test config only.
- **Non-Ecto test isolation.** If your test is purely logic (no DB at all), you don't need this skill — you don't need a sandbox, you don't need `async` tags to wire through Repo config, none of it applies. `use ExUnit.Case, async: true` and go.
- **Mnesia or in-memory-only Repos.** Those don't use DBConnection's ownership pool. Similar isolation story, different mechanism.
- **Cross-node distributed tests.** Sandbox is node-local. If you're testing a multi-node cluster (Horde, Swarm, libcluster), you need a different approach entirely — typically spinning up real databases per node and truncating between tests.
- **Generic flakiness unrelated to the database.** Time-dependent code, random ordering, HTTP mocks — all valid concerns, but not sandbox concerns. §12 mentions them under hypothesis (d) but doesn't go deep.

If the failing test doesn't involve a Repo query that crosses a process boundary, this skill is the wrong tool. Look at `ExUnit.Case` options, `Mox`, `Mimic`, or — if the test is hitting a real external resource — `Bypass` and friends.

- **`hexdocs.pm/ecto/Ecto.Adapters.SQL.html#module-test-support`** — background on how the sandbox wraps the normal DBConnection pool.
- **The BoothIQ post-mortem** — the internal writeup that motivated this skill. Cited inline throughout (§1, §11). The short version: an LLM agent spent two hours debugging a sandbox test, proposed five wrong fixes, and only recovered after a human asked "are you sure you're looking at the test database?" The MCP Audit Protocol in §11 is the direct response.

### Closing note

This document is a stance. Every opinionated claim in it — "`async: true` is the default", "`start_owner!` beats `checkout`", "shared mode is a confession of defeat", "Tidewave is a trap during test debugging" — is defensible, and we think it's correct in 95% of cases. When you find yourself in the 5% where an opinion doesn't fit, write down *why* it doesn't fit. That write-up, tucked into a comment above the test, is the best possible documentation for the next person — human or AI — who reads the code.

The goal is not to memorize every section of this guide. The goal is to hold the connection-ownership state machine (§2) in your head as the default frame, and to know that when a sandbox test fails, the answer is almost always "a pid isn't in the allow-list yet". Everything else is a consequence of that one fact.
supporting_files.scripts/validate.sh:
#!/bin/bash
# validate.sh - Anti-pattern scanner for elixir-ecto-sandbox-test Skill
# Scans a target directory recursively for common Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox
# anti-patterns documented in the elixir-ecto-sandbox-test-composite Skill.
#
# Usage: validate.sh <target_dir>
# Exit codes: 0 if clean, 1 if any hits found
#
# bash 3.2 compatible (macOS default). No associative arrays, no `local -A`.

set -u

TARGET="${1:-.}"

if [ ! -d "$TARGET" ]; then
  echo "validate.sh: target directory not found: $TARGET" >&2
  exit 1
fi

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Counter helpers (bash 3.2 indirect expansion)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

set_count() {
  eval "COUNT_$1=$2"
}

get_count() {
  local name="COUNT_$1"
  eval "printf '%s' \"\${$name:-0}\""
}

bump_count() {
  local cur
  cur=$(get_count "$1")
  set_count "$1" $((cur + 1))
}

TOTAL_HITS=0

if [ -t 1 ]; then
  C_RED=$'\033[31m'
  C_YEL=$'\033[33m'
  C_BLU=$'\033[34m'
  C_GRN=$'\033[32m'
  C_DIM=$'\033[2m'
  C_RST=$'\033[0m'
else
  C_RED=""
  C_YEL=""
  C_BLU=""
  C_GRN=""
  C_DIM=""
  C_RST=""
fi

sev_tag() {
  case "$1" in
    HIGH)   printf '%s[HIGH]%s'   "$C_RED" "$C_RST" ;;
    MEDIUM) printf '%s[MEDIUM]%s' "$C_YEL" "$C_RST" ;;
    LOW)    printf '%s[LOW]%s'    "$C_BLU" "$C_RST" ;;
    *)      printf '[%s]' "$1" ;;
  esac
}

report_detector() {
  local name="$1"
  local sev="$2"
  local hint="$3"
  local any=0
  local line

  printf '\n%s>>> %s%s %s\n' "$C_DIM" "$name" "$C_RST" "$(sev_tag "$sev")"

  while IFS= read -r line; do
    [ -z "$line" ] && continue
    any=1
    bump_count "$name"
    TOTAL_HITS=$((TOTAL_HITS + 1))
    printf '    %s\n' "$line"
  done

  if [ "$any" -eq 0 ]; then
    printf '    %sclean%s\n' "$C_GRN" "$C_RST"
  else
    printf '    %sfix: %s%s\n' "$C_DIM" "$hint" "$C_RST"
  fi
}

list_test_files() {
  find "$TARGET" -type f -name '*_test.exs' 2>/dev/null
}

list_all_ex() {
  find "$TARGET" -type f \( -name '*.ex' -o -name '*.exs' \) 2>/dev/null
}

list_endpoint_files() {
  find "$TARGET/lib" -type f -path '*_web/endpoint.ex' 2>/dev/null
}

d1_auto_mode_in_tests() {
  list_test_files | while IFS= read -r f; do
    grep -nE 'Sandbox\.mode\([^,]+,[[:space:]]*:auto\b' "$f" 2>/dev/null \
      | while IFS=: read -r ln rest; do
          printf '%s:%s: %s\n' "$f" "$ln" ":auto is the production pool mode; tests must use :manual"
        done
  done
}

d2_drain_queue_in_tests() {
  list_test_files | while IFS= read -r f; do
    grep -nE 'Oban\.drain_queue\(' "$f" 2>/dev/null \
      | while IFS=: read -r ln rest; do
          printf '%s:%s: %s\n' "$f" "$ln" "drain_queue runs in another process; jobs silently drain to zero"
        done
  done
}

d3_process_sleep_in_tests() {
  list_test_files | while IFS= read -r f; do
    grep -nE 'Process\.sleep\(' "$f" 2>/dev/null \
      | while IFS=: read -r ln rest; do
          printf '%s:%s: %s\n' "$f" "$ln" "sleep hides timing races; use assert_receive/eventually"
        done
  done
}

d4_async_false_no_justification() {
  list_all_ex | while IFS= read -r f; do
    awk -v path="$f" '
      {
        lines[NR] = $0
      }
      END {
        for (i = 1; i <= NR; i++) {
          line = lines[i]
          if (line ~ /async:[[:space:]]*false/) {
            if (line ~ /#[[:space:]]*async:[[:space:]]*false[[:space:]]*[-—:]/) continue
            if (line ~ /#[[:space:]]*LEDGER/) continue
            j = i - 1
            while (j > 0 && lines[j] ~ /^[[:space:]]*$/) j--
            if (j > 0 && lines[j] ~ /#[[:space:]]*async:[[:space:]]*false[[:space:]]*[-—:]/) continue
            if (j > 0 && lines[j] ~ /#[[:space:]]*LEDGER/) continue
            printf "%s:%d: %s\n", path, i, "async: false without `# async: false — <reason>` comment"
          }
        }
      }
    ' "$f"
  done
}

d5_checkout_with_spawns() {
  list_test_files | while IFS= read -r f; do
    awk -v path="$f" '
      /Ecto\.Adapters\.SQL\.Sandbox\.checkout\(/ || /Sandbox\.checkout\(/ {
        checkout_ln = NR
      }
      /Task\.Supervisor\.async_nolink|start_supervised!|GenServer\.start_link/ {
        if (checkout_ln > 0 && NR > checkout_ln) {
          printf "%s:%d: %s\n", path, NR, "checkout/1 + spawned process: use Sandbox.start_owner!/2 instead"
          checkout_ln = 0
        }
      }
    ' "$f"
  done
}

d6_shared_without_ledger() {
  list_all_ex | while IFS= read -r f; do
    awk -v path="$f" '
      {
        lines[NR] = $0
      }
      END {
        for (i = 1; i <= NR; i++) {
          if (lines[i] ~ /Sandbox\.mode\(/ && lines[i] ~ /:shared/) {
            found = 0
            low = (i - 5 >= 1) ? i - 5 : 1
            for (j = low; j <= i; j++) {
              if (lines[j] ~ /#[[:space:]]*LEDGER:/) found = 1
              if (lines[j] ~ /#[[:space:]]*shared:/) found = 1
              if (lines[j] ~ /#[[:space:]]*async:[[:space:]]*false/) found = 1
            }
            if (!found) {
              printf "%s:%d: %s\n", path, i, "shared mode without # LEDGER: / # async: false comment"
            }
          }
        }
      }
    ' "$f"
  done
}

d7_oban_test_config() {
  local cfg="$TARGET/config/test.exs"
  [ -f "$cfg" ] || return 0

  if grep -qE 'Oban' "$cfg" 2>/dev/null; then
    if ! grep -qE 'testing:[[:space:]]*:manual' "$cfg" 2>/dev/null; then
      local ln
      ln=$(grep -nE 'Oban' "$cfg" | head -1 | cut -d: -f1)
      [ -z "$ln" ] && ln=1
      printf '%s:%s: %s\n' "$cfg" "$ln" "Oban configured without testing: :manual"
    fi
  fi
}

d8_liveview_missing_sql_sandbox_plug() {
  local endpoints
  endpoints=$(list_endpoint_files)

  local has_plug=0
  if [ -n "$endpoints" ]; then
    local ep
    for ep in $endpoints; do
      if grep -qE 'plug[[:space:]]+Phoenix\.Ecto\.SQL\.Sandbox' "$ep" 2>/dev/null; then
        has_plug=1
        break
      fi
    done
  fi

  if [ "$has_plug" -eq 1 ]; then
    return 0
  fi

  list_test_files | while IFS= read -r f; do
    if grep -qE 'Phoenix\.LiveViewTest|import[[:space:]]+Phoenix\.LiveViewTest' "$f" 2>/dev/null; then
      if grep -qE '^\s*test[[:space:]]+"' "$f" 2>/dev/null; then
        local ln
        ln=$(grep -nE 'Phoenix\.LiveViewTest' "$f" | head -1 | cut -d: -f1)
        [ -z "$ln" ] && ln=1
        printf '%s:%s: %s\n' "$f" "$ln" "LiveView tests present but endpoint missing plug Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox"
      fi
    fi
  done
}

d9_connect_info_missing_user_agent() {
  list_endpoint_files | while IFS= read -r f; do
    awk -v path="$f" '
      /socket[[:space:]]+"/ { in_socket = 1; socket_start = NR }
      in_socket && /connect_info:/ {
        block = $0
        depth = gsub(/\[/, "[", block) - gsub(/\]/, "]", block)
        start = NR
        while (depth > 0 && (getline nextline) > 0) {
          block = block "\n" nextline
          depth += gsub(/\[/, "[", nextline) - gsub(/\]/, "]", nextline)
          if (NR - start > 40) break
        }
        if (block !~ /:user_agent/) {
          printf "%s:%d: %s\n", path, start, "socket connect_info missing :user_agent (needed by Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox)"
        }
        in_socket = 0
      }
      /^\s*end\s*$/ { in_socket = 0 }
    ' "$f"
  done
}

d10_tidewave_in_tests() {
  list_test_files | while IFS= read -r f; do
    grep -niE 'tidewave' "$f" 2>/dev/null \
      | while IFS=: read -r ln rest; do
          printf '%s:%s: %s\n' "$f" "$ln" "tidewave reference in test — tests hit the test DB, not the dev DB"
        done
  done
}

d11_put_env_in_setup() {
  list_test_files | while IFS= read -r f; do
    awk -v path="$f" '
      /^\s*use[[:space:]]+/ && /async:[[:space:]]*false/ { async_false = 1 }
      /^\s*use[[:space:]]+/ && /async:[[:space:]]*true/  { async_false = 0; async_true = 1 }
      /^\s*setup[[:space:]]+do\b/ { in_setup = 1; setup_depth = 1; next }
      in_setup {
        nopen = gsub(/\bdo\b/, "&")
        nend  = gsub(/\bend\b/, "&")
        setup_depth += nopen - nend
        if (/Application\.put_env\(/ && !async_false) {
          printf "%s:%d: %s\n", path, NR, "Application.put_env in setup without async: false — global state leak"
        }
        if (setup_depth <= 0) in_setup = 0
      }
    ' "$f"
  done
}

d12_allow_after_query() {
  list_test_files | while IFS= read -r f; do
    awk -v path="$f" '
      /Repo\.(all|one|get|insert|update|delete|aggregate|query|preload|transaction)/ {
        if (repo_ln == 0) repo_ln = NR
      }
      /Sandbox\.allow\(/ {
        if (repo_ln > 0 && NR > repo_ln) {
          printf "%s:%d: %s\n", path, NR, "Sandbox.allow/3 called after Repo.* query — allow before first query"
        }
      }
    ' "$f"
  done
}

printf '%s================================================================%s\n' "$C_DIM" "$C_RST"
printf '  elixir-ecto-sandbox-test  :: anti-pattern scanner\n'
printf '  target: %s\n' "$TARGET"
printf '%s================================================================%s\n' "$C_DIM" "$C_RST"

report_detector "AUTO_MODE_IN_TESTS"           "HIGH"   \
  "Use Sandbox.mode(Repo, :manual) in config/test.exs; never :auto" \
  < <(d1_auto_mode_in_tests)

report_detector "DRAIN_QUEUE_IN_TESTS"         "HIGH"   \
  "Use perform_job/2 or Oban.Testing assertions; drain_queue drains in another proc" \
  < <(d2_drain_queue_in_tests)

report_detector "PROCESS_SLEEP_IN_TESTS"       "MEDIUM" \
  "Replace with assert_receive or an eventually/retry helper" \
  < <(d3_process_sleep_in_tests)

report_detector "ASYNC_FALSE_NO_JUSTIFY"       "LOW"    \
  "Add '# async: false — <reason>' on the line above the use" \
  < <(d4_async_false_no_justification)

report_detector "CHECKOUT_WITH_SPAWNED_PROC"   "MEDIUM" \
  "Switch to Sandbox.start_owner!(Repo, shared: ...) + on_exit stop_owner" \
  < <(d5_checkout_with_spawns)

report_detector "SHARED_WITHOUT_LEDGER"        "LOW"    \
  "Add '# LEDGER: <reason>' + '# async: false' above the shared mode call" \
  < <(d6_shared_without_ledger)

report_detector "OBAN_TEST_NOT_MANUAL"         "LOW"    \
  "Set config :my_app, Oban, testing: :manual in config/test.exs" \
  < <(d7_oban_test_config)

report_detector "LV_MISSING_SQL_SANDBOX_PLUG"  "HIGH"   \
  "Add plug Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox, at: \"/sandbox\" guarded by Mix.env()" \
  < <(d8_liveview_missing_sql_sandbox_plug)

report_detector "CONNECT_INFO_MISSING_UA"      "MEDIUM" \
  "Add :user_agent to socket connect_info: [session: ..., user_agent: true]" \
  < <(d9_connect_info_missing_user_agent)

report_detector "TIDEWAVE_IN_TESTS"            "LOW"    \
  "Tidewave is a dev-DB MCP; tests always run against Repo+test DB" \
  < <(d10_tidewave_in_tests)

report_detector "PUT_ENV_IN_SETUP"             "MEDIUM" \
  "Move to fixtures, or tag the test module async: false with justification" \
  < <(d11_put_env_in_setup)

report_detector "ALLOW_AFTER_QUERY"            "MEDIUM" \
  "Call Sandbox.allow/3 before the first Repo.* call; ordering matters" \
  < <(d12_allow_after_query)

printf '\n%s================================================================%s\n' "$C_DIM" "$C_RST"
printf '  TOTAL_HITS: %d\n' "$TOTAL_HITS"
printf '%s================================================================%s\n' "$C_DIM" "$C_RST"

if [ "$TOTAL_HITS" -eq 0 ]; then
  printf '  %sclean%s\n' "$C_GRN" "$C_RST"
  exit 0
else
  exit 1
fi
supporting_files.test_fixtures/async_shared_contradiction:
# fixture: Test module that declares async: true but uses shared mode — self-contradiction
defmodule MyApp.ReportGeneratorTest do
  use MyApp.DataCase, async: true

  alias MyApp.Accounts
  alias MyApp.Reports

  setup do
    # BUG: we're forcing shared mode AND async: true on the use line above.
    # Shared mode requires async: false because the owner must outlive every
    # allowed worker — async tests run concurrently, so multiple owners would
    # compete for the single shared pool.
    :ok = Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.checkout(MyApp.Repo)
    Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.mode(MyApp.Repo, {:shared, self()})
    :ok
  end

  test "generates a report" do
    {:ok, _user} = Accounts.create_user(%{email: "r@example.com"})
    assert %{user_count: 1} = Reports.generate_summary()
  end
end

# fixture: DataCase template with subtle ordering bug — mode(:shared) called before checkout
defmodule MyApp.DataCase do
  use ExUnit.CaseTemplate

  using do
    quote do
      alias MyApp.Repo
      import Ecto
      import Ecto.Changeset
      import Ecto.Query
      import MyApp.DataCase
    end
  end

  setup tags do
    # BUG: mode(:shared) before checkout — the owner isn't established yet,
    # so subsequent checkouts go through a GenServer with no owner to share.
    Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.mode(MyApp.Repo, {:shared, self()})
    :ok = Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.checkout(MyApp.Repo)

    :ok
  end
end

# fixture: Phoenix Channel missing sandbox allow — channel process has no DB connection
defmodule MyAppWeb.RoomChannel do
  use Phoenix.Channel

  alias MyApp.Rooms
  alias MyApp.Accounts

  @impl true
  def join("room:" <> room_id, _params, socket) do
    # BUG: This channel process needs DB access via Rooms.get_room!/1, but
    # there is no Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox.allow/3 call to grant the channel
    # process the sandbox allowance from the test process.
    case Rooms.get_room(room_id) do
      nil ->
        {:error, %{reason: "not_found"}}

      room ->
        {:ok, assign(socket, :room, room)}
    end
  end

  @impl true
  def handle_in("new_msg", %{"body" => body}, socket) do
    user = Accounts.get_user!(socket.assigns.user_id)
    {:ok, msg} = Rooms.create_message(socket.assigns.room, user, body)
    broadcast!(socket, "new_msg", %{body: msg.body, user_email: user.email})
    {:noreply, socket}
  end
end
supporting_files.test_fixtures/data_case_no_async_handlin:
# fixture: DataCase that checks out sandbox but does not handle async tag — every test forced to serial
defmodule MyApp.DataCase do
  use ExUnit.CaseTemplate

  using do
    quote do
      alias MyApp.Repo
      import Ecto
      import Ecto.Changeset
      import Ecto.Query
      import MyApp.DataCase
    end
  end

  setup _tags do
    :ok = Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.checkout(MyApp.Repo)
    # MISSING: `shared: not tags[:async]` pattern — never calls mode/2, so all tests
    # must be serial to avoid cross-process ownership errors when code spawns workers.
    Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.mode(MyApp.Repo, {:shared, self()})
    :ok
  end

  def errors_on(changeset) do
    Ecto.Changeset.traverse_errors(changeset, fn {message, opts} ->
      Regex.replace(~r"%{(\w+)}", message, fn _, key ->
        opts |> Keyword.get(String.to_existing_atom(key), key) |> to_string()
      end)
    end)
  end
end
supporting_files.test_fixtures/dynamic_supervisor_test.ex:
# fixture: Test starts a DynamicSupervisor and spawns children that need DB access
# Uses the wrong PID for allow/3 (passes the supervisor PID, not the worker PID)
defmodule MyApp.JobRunnerTest do
  use MyApp.DataCase, async: true

  alias MyApp.JobRunner
  alias MyApp.Accounts

  test "runs a job against a user record" do
    {:ok, user} = Accounts.create_user(%{email: "jobs@example.com"})

    {:ok, supervisor_pid} = DynamicSupervisor.start_link(strategy: :one_for_one)

    # BUG: allow/3 is called on the SUPERVISOR pid, not the child worker pid.
    # The supervisor never performs DB operations; the child does. The child
    # spawned via start_child/2 will fail with a connection ownership error.
    Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.allow(MyApp.Repo, self(), supervisor_pid)

    {:ok, _child_pid} =
      DynamicSupervisor.start_child(
        supervisor_pid,
        {JobRunner, user_id: user.id}
      )

    assert_receive {:job_complete, _}, 1_000
  end
end
supporting_files.test_fixtures/flaky_genserver_test.ex:
# fixture: Flaky test — GenServer started via start_link outlives the test process
defmodule MyApp.CounterServerTest do
  use MyApp.DataCase, async: true

  alias MyApp.CounterServer
  alias MyApp.Metrics

  test "increments a counter and records a metric" do
    # BUG: start_link creates a process unlinked from the test process.
    # When the test returns, the GenServer is still alive — and the sandbox
    # owner (the test process) has already relinquished the connection,
    # so the GenServer's next Repo call produces an ownership error that
    # manifests as "cannot find ownership process for #PID<...>".
    {:ok, pid} = CounterServer.start_link(name: :my_counter)

    CounterServer.increment(pid)
    CounterServer.increment(pid)

    # CounterServer writes a metric via Repo.insert — runs in the GenServer process,
    # which was never `allow/3`-ed.
    assert Metrics.total_increments() == 2
  end
end

# fixture: Endpoint module with Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox plug in wrong position
defmodule MyAppWeb.Endpoint do
  use Phoenix.Endpoint, otp_app: :my_app

  @session_options [
    store: :cookie,
    key: "_my_app_key",
    signing_salt: "abcdefgh",
    same_site: "Lax"
  ]

  socket "/live", Phoenix.LiveView.Socket,
    websocket: [connect_info: [session: @session_options]]

  plug Plug.Static,
    at: "/",
    from: :my_app,
    gzip: false,
    only: MyAppWeb.static_paths()

  plug Plug.RequestId
  plug Plug.Telemetry, event_prefix: [:phoenix, :endpoint]

  plug Plug.Parsers,
    parsers: [:urlencoded, :multipart, :json],
    pass: ["*/*"],
    json_decoder: Phoenix.json_library()

  plug Plug.MethodOverride
  plug Plug.Head
  plug Plug.Session, @session_options

  # BUG: sandbox plug placed here is too late — must be at the top of endpoint.ex
  # before other plugs so metadata propagates to every request and LV socket.
  if Application.compile_env(:my_app, :sql_sandbox, false) do
    plug Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox
  end

  plug MyAppWeb.Router
end
