# Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox Plug

## Quick Start
LiveView runs in its own process. The sandbox plug shares ownership via a header set by `Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox.metadata_for/2`.

## Workflow
1. Add `Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox` plug to the endpoint
2. In Conn tests, set the header using `Sandbox.metadata_for/2`
3. LiveViewTest automatically honors the metadata

frontmatter.description: Tests Oban workers synchronously via perform_job/3 so they can see sandboxed data. NOT for integration tests with real queue.
frontmatter.name: oban-sandbox-integration--perform-job

skill_genomes.skill_md_content:
---
name: oban-sandbox-integration--perform-job
description: Tests Oban workers synchronously via perform_job/3 so they can see sandboxed data. NOT for integration tests with real queue.
---

# perform_job In Sandbox

## Quick Start
`perform_job/3` runs the worker in the test process, which sees the sandbox data. `drain_queue` runs in a different process, which does NOT.

## Workflow
1. `use Oban.Testing, repo: MyApp.Repo`
2. Call `perform_job(Worker, %{"key" => val})` with string-keyed args
3. Never use `Oban.drain_queue/1` in sandbox tests

frontmatter.description: Ecto sandbox checkout and mode selection for test isolation. Use when configuring MyApp.DataCase, deciding between :manual and :shared mode, or reviewing test setup for correctness. NOT for production DB pools.
frontmatter.name: sandbox-checkout-and-modes--explicit-owner

skill_genomes.skill_md_content:
---
name: sandbox-checkout-and-modes--explicit-owner
description: Ecto sandbox checkout and mode selection for test isolation. Use when configuring MyApp.DataCase, deciding between :manual and :shared mode, or reviewing test setup for correctness. NOT for production DB pools.
---

# Explicit Owner Lifecycle

## Quick Start
`start_owner!/2` replaces the legacy `checkout/2`. Always pair with `stop_owner/1`.

## Workflow
1. In setup: `pid = Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.start_owner!(Repo, shared: not tags[:async])`
2. Register teardown: `on_exit(fn -> Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.stop_owner(pid) end)`
3. Mode: `:manual` is default; `:shared` only when needed (sync tests); NEVER `:auto`

## Common mistakes
- Using `Sandbox.mode(:auto)` in test code
- Forgetting `stop_owner` in on_exit

frontmatter.description: Uses shared mode as a documented fallback when per-process isolation is not viable. Not a default.
frontmatter.name: shared-mode-fallback--justified

skill_genomes.skill_md_content:
---
name: shared-mode-fallback--justified
description: Uses shared mode as a documented fallback when per-process isolation is not viable. Not a default.
---

# Justified Shared Mode

## Quick Start
Shared mode means every process sees the same sandboxed connection. It's a fallback, not a default.

## Workflow
1. Default to per-process isolation
2. If you MUST use shared mode, call `Sandbox.mode(Repo, {:shared, self()})` in setup
3. Add a comment explaining why (e.g., "GenServer under test writes via a different process we can't allow/3")

frontmatter.description: Enforces per-test transaction isolation via Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.start_owner! with the canonical shared: not tags[:async] pattern. Use when writing Phoenix/Elixir test case templates, debugging test bleed, or diagnosing flaky tests that pass alone but fail in the suite. NOT for production DB connection pooling — this is test-only.
frontmatter.name: test-isolation-philosophy--per-test-transaction

skill_genomes.skill_md_content:
---
name: test-isolation-philosophy--per-test-transaction
description: Enforces per-test transaction isolation via Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.start_owner! with the canonical shared: not tags[:async] pattern. Use when writing Phoenix/Elixir test case templates, debugging test bleed, or diagnosing flaky tests that pass alone but fail in the suite. NOT for production DB connection pooling — this is test-only.
---

# Per-Test Transaction Isolation

## Quick Start
Every Ecto test opens a sandboxed transaction that is rolled back on exit. The case template is the seam where this gets wired up.

## When to use
- Writing `MyApp.DataCase` or any custom case template
- Debugging "passes alone, fails in suite" bugs
- Fixing tests that leak state across runs
- Reviewing PRs that touch test setup

## Workflow
1. Use `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.start_owner!(MyApp.Repo, shared: not tags[:async])`
2. Register `on_exit(fn -> Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.stop_owner(pid) end)`
3. Keep `async: true` on tests that can run concurrently
4. For spawned processes, call `Sandbox.allow/3` BEFORE spawning

## Examples
```elixir
defmodule MyApp.DataCase do
  use ExUnit.CaseTemplate

  setup tags do
    pid = Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.start_owner!(MyApp.Repo, shared: not tags[:async])
    on_exit(fn -> Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.stop_owner(pid) end)
    :ok
  end
end
```

## Common mistakes
- Using `Sandbox.mode(Repo, :auto)` in test code (breaks isolation)
- Setting `async: false` to mask an ownership bug
- Seeding the dev DB "so tests have data"

frontmatter.description: Prevents the Tidewave dev-vs-test DB confusion. Explicit sandbox ownership, never fall back to seeding the dev DB.
frontmatter.name: tidewave-dev-vs-test-trap--explicit-ownership

skill_genomes.skill_md_content:
---
name: tidewave-dev-vs-test-trap--explicit-ownership
description: Prevents the Tidewave dev-vs-test DB confusion. Explicit sandbox ownership, never fall back to seeding the dev DB.
---

# Explicit Test Ownership

## Quick Start
The sandboxed Repo in tests is a separate connection from Tidewave's dev Repo. Don't conflate them.

## Common mistakes
- Seeding the dev DB "so tests can see data"
- Trying to read test results via Tidewave's dev connection
- Assuming Repo in tests and Repo in dev share state

frontmatter.description: Catalog of Elixir/Phoenix spawn sites (Task, GenServer, Oban, LiveView) with correct Sandbox.allow/3 placement and race failure modes. Use when test code spawns processes that touch Repo. NOT for pure in-test queries or production bypass.
frontmatter.name: allow-pattern-for-spawned-processes--spawn-site-catalog

skill_genomes.skill_md_content:
---
name: allow-pattern-for-spawned-processes--spawn-site-catalog
description: Catalog of Elixir/Phoenix spawn sites (Task, GenServer, Oban, LiveView) with correct Sandbox.allow/3 placement and race failure modes. Use when test code spawns processes that touch Repo. NOT for pure in-test queries or production bypass.
---

# Spawn-Site Catalog for Sandbox.allow/3

## Quick Start
Every new pid that touches `Repo` needs an `allow/3` call placed **before** the child issues its first query. Find the spawn site, pick the matching recipe from the catalog, and hand off the parent pid.

## When to use
Use this skill when a test case spawns any of the following and gets `DBConnection.OwnershipError`:
- `Task.async/1`, `Task.start_link/1`
- `Task.Supervisor.async_nolink/2`, `Task.Supervisor.async_stream/3`
- `spawn/1`, `spawn_link/1`
- `DynamicSupervisor.start_child/2`
- `GenServer.start_link/3` (both test-started and app-started)
- `Oban.Testing.perform_job/3` with `:inline` off
- `Phoenix.LiveView` mount in a `LiveViewTest` session

**NOT for**: pure in-test queries (the test pid already owns the connection), ExUnit `setup` blocks that run in the test pid, or any attempt to bypass sandboxing in production.

## The Handoff Rule
```
parent_pid   ──owns──▶  sandboxed connection
     │
     │  (1) spawn child
     ▼
  child_pid  ──needs──▶ connection (not yet!)
     │
     │  (2) parent calls Sandbox.allow(Repo, parent_pid, child_pid)
     ▼
  child_pid  ──allowed──▶ same connection  (3) child queries safely
```
If (2) happens after (3), the child hits `OwnershipError`. The race is always: *"did I allow before the child ran its first query?"*

## Workflow
1. **Find the spawn site** — grep the failing test for `Task.`, `spawn`, `start_link`, `start_child`, `perform_job`, or `live(conn, ...)`.
2. **Capture `self()`** in the test body before spawning: `parent = self()`.
3. **Pick the catalog entry** matching the spawn primitive (below).
4. **Insert `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.allow(Repo, parent, child_pid)`** at the exact spot the recipe specifies.
5. **Run the test** — if it still fails, the child is spawning a grandchild. Recurse: every new pid in the tree needs its own allow.

## Catalog

| Spawn site | Where to call `allow/3` | Failure mode if placed wrong |
|---|---|---|
| `Task.async/1` | After `Task.async`, before `Task.await` | Child races parent; query fires before allow lands |
| `Task.Supervisor.async_nolink` | Immediately after the call returns the `%Task{}` | Detached task queries before allow registers |
| `Task.Supervisor.async_stream` | Inside the mapper, via a `start_fun` that blocks on allow | Stream workers spin up in parallel; any lagging allow loses |
| `spawn_link` | Inside a synchronization pattern (child waits on `:go` message) | Child runs arbitrary code immediately — no window to allow |
| `DynamicSupervisor.start_child` | After `start_child/2` returns `{:ok, pid}`, before triggering work | Child's `init/1` may already query the Repo |
| `GenServer.start_link` (test-started) | After `start_link`, before first `call`/`cast` | `handle_continue` or `init/1` queries before allow |
| `GenServer` (app-started) | In test `setup`, against the registered pid | App-started server is long-lived; stale ownership persists |
| `Oban` job (non-inline) | Use `Oban.Testing.with_testing_mode(:manual, ...)` + `perform_job/3` | Background executor owns a different connection |
| `Phoenix.LiveView` mount | Set `:shared` mode in `setup`, not `allow/3` | LiveView mount races; per-pid allow can't keep up with reconnects |

## Examples

### Example 1: Task.async with explicit handoff
```elixir
defmodule MyApp.BackgroundTaskTest do
  use MyApp.DataCase, async: true
  alias Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox
  alias MyApp.{Repo, Accounts}

  test "background enrichment writes to DB" do
    parent = self()
    user = insert(:user)

    task =
      Task.async(fn ->
        # Child blocks until parent has granted access.
        receive do
          :allowed -> :ok
        end

        Accounts.enrich_user(user.id)
      end)

    # Step (2): hand off BEFORE releasing the child.
    :ok = Sandbox.allow(Repo, parent, task.pid)
    send(task.pid, :allowed)

    assert :ok = Task.await(task)
    assert Repo.get!(User, user.id).enriched == true
  end
end
```

### Example 2: GenServer with `handle_continue` that queries on boot
```elixir
defmodule MyApp.CacheWarmerTest do
  use MyApp.DataCase, async: false
  alias Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox
  alias MyApp.{Repo, CacheWarmer}

  setup do
    parent = self()

    # Start the server but keep it idle via an :init_mode option that
    # skips the DB-touching handle_continue until we grant access.
    {:ok, pid} = CacheWarmer.start_link(init_mode: :wait, parent: parent)

    # Hand off the connection to the freshly-born child pid.
    :ok = Sandbox.allow(Repo, parent, pid)

    # Now tell it to proceed — the DB query inside handle_continue will
    # find an owning pid in the allow table.
    :ok = CacheWarmer.proceed(pid)

    {:ok, cache: pid}
  end

  test "cache warmer loads products", %{cache: pid} do
    insert_list(3, :product)
    assert CacheWarmer.refresh(pid) == :ok
    assert CacheWarmer.size(pid) == 3
  end
end
```

## Common mistakes
- **Allowing after the child has already queried.** The handoff has to happen before the child's first `Repo` call. Use an explicit sync (`receive`/`send`, `GenServer.call`, or a blocked init) to create the window.
- **Forgetting grandchildren.** A `Task` that itself spawns a `Task` needs a second `allow/3`. Every new pid in the tree is its own ownership unit.
- **Using `allow/3` in `async: true` tests against an app-started GenServer.** The shared long-lived pid will hold ownership across unrelated tests. Either switch the test to `async: false` and use `:shared` sandbox mode, or restart the server per-test.
- **Reaching for `Sandbox.mode(Repo, :shared)` as a first response.** Shared mode breaks test parallelism. Prefer per-pid `allow/3` and only fall back to `:shared` for LiveView or when the spawn site is genuinely unreachable from the test pid.
- **Calling `allow/3` from inside the child.** The child doesn't own the connection yet, so it can't grant itself access. `allow/3` must be called from a pid that already owns (or is already allowed on) the connection — usually the test pid.

frontmatter.description: Enforces async-by-default with a written justification ladder before any test falls off async. Use when writing Ecto tests, reviewing PRs with async:false, or auditing suite flakiness. NOT for production code or non-Ecto projects.
frontmatter.name: async-test-safety-rules--default-async-justified

skill_genomes.skill_md_content:
---
name: async-test-safety-rules--default-async-justified
description: Enforces async-by-default with a written justification ladder before any test falls off async. Use when writing Ecto tests, reviewing PRs with async:false, or auditing suite flakiness. NOT for production code or non-Ecto projects.
---

# Default Async With Justification Ladder

## Quick Start
Every test is `async: true` by default. Falling off async is a **declared exception**, not a default. Before accepting `async: false`, the author must name their justification from the ladder below and write it as a comment on the `use` line. No justification, no merge.

## When to use
Use when:
- Writing any new Ecto-backed test module
- Reviewing a PR that adds or keeps `async: false`
- Debugging suite-level flakiness and tempted to "fix" it by serializing tests
- Auditing a test suite for drifted `async: false` rot

Do NOT use when:
- The module has no DB involvement at all (plain unit tests — use `ExUnit.Case` without SQL sandbox)
- You are wiring sandbox setup itself (that is the foundation's job)
- You are changing production code

## The Justification Ladder
Ranked from **most acceptable** (top) to **least acceptable** (bottom). Everything below the line is a smell.

1. **Global Mox mode** — `Mox.set_mox_global()` in setup. Mox rewires module dispatch process-globally; parallel tests would clobber each other. Acceptable but prefer `Mox.set_mox_from_context/1` + `verify_on_exit!/1` first.
2. **`Application.put_env/3` mutation** — the test flips a global config key and restores it on exit. Parallel tests would race. Acceptable if the config genuinely cannot be injected.
3. **Named GenServer under test** — the SUT is a singleton registered by name (`Process.whereis/1`). Two async tests would both write to the same server. Acceptable but consider `start_supervised!/1` with a unique name instead.
4. **Non-sandboxed external resource** — a real external process (filesystem lock, port, embedded DB) the test must own exclusively. Last resort. Document the resource by name.

--- below this line is **NEVER** acceptable ---

5. **"The test was flaky"** — flakiness is a symptom. The cause is almost always missing `Sandbox.allow/3` between the test pid and a child pid (Task, GenServer, Oban worker). Fix ownership. Do not serialize.
6. **"It touches the DB"** — every test in this family touches the DB. That is what the sandbox is for.
7. **"Other tests in the file are async: false"** — cargo cult. Each module decides on its own evidence.

## Workflow
1. **Start async.** Every new test module opens with `use MyApp.DataCase, async: true`. No exceptions in the first draft.
2. **Run the test.** If it passes, you are done. Commit it.
3. **If it fails intermittently**, do NOT flip to `async: false`. Instead, trace the failing query to its pid and ask: "Is this pid the test pid, or a child spawned by the test?" If it is a child, the fix is `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.allow(Repo, self(), child_pid)` — not serialization.
4. **If the test genuinely requires global state**, pick the highest-ranked justification from the ladder that applies, then add a comment on the `use` line:
   ```elixir
   # async: false — justification #1 (Mox global mode for cross-process dispatch)
   use MyApp.DataCase, async: false
   ```
5. **In review**, reject any `async: false` that lacks a numbered justification comment, or that cites justification #5, #6, or #7.
6. **Periodically audit** the suite: `grep -rn "async: false" test/` and cross-check each hit against its comment. Stale justifications get cleaned up.

## Examples

### Wrongly marked async: false (the shotgun fix)
```elixir
# BEFORE — author flipped async: false because "the test was flaky"
defmodule MyApp.OrderProcessorTest do
  use MyApp.DataCase, async: false  # no justification comment — smell

  test "processes order in background task" do
    order = insert(:order)

    Task.start(fn ->
      MyApp.OrderProcessor.process(order.id)
    end)

    Process.sleep(100)
    assert Repo.get!(Order, order.id).status == "processed"
  end
end
```
The real bug: the `Task` runs in a child pid that does not own the sandbox connection. The Repo query inside the Task raises `DBConnection.OwnershipError`, the test flakes on timing, and the author "fixed" it by serializing the entire module. Every other test in the file now pays the cost.

```elixir
# AFTER — async restored, ownership granted explicitly
defmodule MyApp.OrderProcessorTest do
  use MyApp.DataCase, async: true

  test "processes order in background task" do
    order = insert(:order)
    parent = self()

    {:ok, task_pid} = Task.start(fn ->
      receive do
        :go ->
          MyApp.OrderProcessor.process(order.id)
          send(parent, :done)
      end
    end)

    Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.allow(Repo, self(), task_pid)
    send(task_pid, :go)
    assert_receive :done, 1_000

    assert Repo.get!(Order, order.id).status == "processed"
  end
end
```

### Legitimate async: false with a ladder citation
```elixir
defmodule MyApp.FeatureFlagTest do
  # async: false — justification #2 (Application.put_env for :feature_flags global)
  use MyApp.DataCase, async: false

  setup do
    prior = Application.get_env(:my_app, :feature_flags)
    Application.put_env(:my_app, :feature_flags, %{new_checkout: true})
    on_exit(fn -> Application.put_env(:my_app, :feature_flags, prior) end)
    :ok
  end

  test "new checkout path is taken when flag is on" do
    assert MyApp.Checkout.path() == :new
  end
end
```
This one is honest: it mutates `Application` state, it names the justification, and review can verify the claim in seconds.

## Common mistakes
- **Flipping `async: false` to silence a flake.** The flake is telling you a child pid lacks ownership. Listen to it.
- **Omitting the justification comment.** "I know why it is async: false" is not a review artifact. Write it down.
- **Citing justification #5 ("the test was flaky").** This is a non-justification. Find the real cause.
- **Module-wide `async: false` when only one test needs it.** Split the offending test into its own module; keep the rest async.
- **Assuming `async: false` is free.** One serialized module blocks one scheduler core for its entire duration. At 200 modules it is minutes of wall-clock cost.
- **Treating the ladder as advisory.** It is a merge gate. Reviews enforce it or the suite rots within a quarter.

frontmatter.description: Transfers Ecto sandbox ownership across multiple Phoenix channels, Presence trackers, and shards in one test. Use when orchestrating two-user chats, room broadcasts, or Presence-tracked sessions. NOT for single-channel or non-DB channel tests.
frontmatter.name: channels-sandbox-integration--multi-channel-choreography

skill_genomes.skill_md_content:
---
name: channels-sandbox-integration--multi-channel-choreography
description: Transfers Ecto sandbox ownership across multiple Phoenix channels, Presence trackers, and shards in one test. Use when orchestrating two-user chats, room broadcasts, or Presence-tracked sessions. NOT for single-channel or non-DB channel tests.
---

# Multi-Channel Choreography

## Quick Start
When a test spins up more than one channel process (or Presence, which spawns its own tracker pid), you must `Sandbox.allow/3` **each new pid exactly once** from the test pid. Centralize this in a `ChannelCase` helper so every `join/3` auto-allows, and guard against the `:already_shared` / "cannot allow twice" error by tracking which pids have already been granted.

## When to use
Use this variant when your test needs DB access from:
- Two or more channel sockets in the same test (e.g., `userA_socket` and `userB_socket` both hitting `RoomChannel`)
- `Phoenix.Presence.track/3` (spawns its own pid under the tracker supervisor)
- Channels that enqueue Oban/Task work that touches the same Repo
- Multi-shard setups where each shard needs independent ownership

Do NOT use when:
- Only one channel is under test — use the simpler `allow-on-channel` variant
- The channel never touches the Repo (pure in-memory broadcast)
- You are running in `:shared` sandbox mode — ownership is automatic there

## Workflow
1. Define a `ChannelCase` that overrides `subscribe_and_join/3` to capture the channel pid and call `allow_channel/1` automatically.
2. In `allow_channel/1`, look up the channel pid via `socket.channel_pid` (post-join) and call `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.allow(Repo, self(), channel_pid)` — but only if not already allowed.
3. Maintain a per-test MapSet of allowed pids in the process dictionary (`:sandbox_allowed_pids`) to prevent `:already_shared` errors on the second `allow/3`.
4. After `Presence.track/3`, resolve the tracker pid with `Process.whereis(MyAppWeb.Presence)` and allow it the same way.
5. For multi-shard tests, repeat steps 2-4 for each `Repo` module (`Repo.Primary`, `Repo.Replica`, etc.).
6. In CI, channel startup is slower — wrap the first DB-touching assertion in `assert_receive` or `Process.sleep(10)` to let the channel finish joining before the parent yields.
7. Never call `allow/3` from inside the channel's own `handle_info` — ownership must be granted **before** the channel touches the Repo.

## Examples

```elixir
# test/support/channel_case.ex
defmodule MyAppWeb.ChannelCase do
  use ExUnit.CaseTemplate

  using do
    quote do
      import Phoenix.ChannelTest
      import MyAppWeb.ChannelCase

      @endpoint MyAppWeb.Endpoint
    end
  end

  setup tags do
    pid = Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.start_owner!(MyApp.Repo, shared: not tags[:async])
    on_exit(fn -> Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.stop_owner(pid) end)
    Process.put(:sandbox_allowed_pids, MapSet.new([self()]))
    :ok
  end

  @doc "Join a channel and auto-allow its pid on the sandbox."
  def join_and_allow(socket, topic, params \\ %{}) do
    {:ok, reply, socket} = Phoenix.ChannelTest.subscribe_and_join(socket, topic, params)
    allow_pid(socket.channel_pid)
    {:ok, reply, socket}
  end

  @doc "Idempotent allow — safe to call multiple times per pid."
  def allow_pid(pid) when is_pid(pid) do
    allowed = Process.get(:sandbox_allowed_pids, MapSet.new())

    unless MapSet.member?(allowed, pid) do
      Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.allow(MyApp.Repo, self(), pid)
      # Presence tracker shares the same sandbox owner
      if tracker = Process.whereis(MyAppWeb.Presence) do
        unless MapSet.member?(allowed, tracker) do
          Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.allow(MyApp.Repo, self(), tracker)
          Process.put(:sandbox_allowed_pids, MapSet.put(allowed, tracker))
        end
      end
      Process.put(:sandbox_allowed_pids, MapSet.put(allowed, pid))
    end

    :ok
  end
end
```

```elixir
# test/my_app_web/channels/room_channel_test.exs
defmodule MyAppWeb.RoomChannelTest do
  use MyAppWeb.ChannelCase, async: true

  alias MyApp.{Accounts, Chat}

  setup do
    {:ok, alice} = Accounts.create_user(%{email: "alice@test"})
    {:ok, bob}   = Accounts.create_user(%{email: "bob@test"})

    {:ok, _, alice_socket} =
      MyAppWeb.UserSocket
      |> socket("user:#{alice.id}", %{user_id: alice.id})
      |> join_and_allow("room:lobby", %{})

    {:ok, _, bob_socket} =
      MyAppWeb.UserSocket
      |> socket("user:#{bob.id}", %{user_id: bob.id})
      |> join_and_allow("room:lobby", %{})

    # Presence.track spawns its own pid — re-run allow_pid to cover it
    :ok = MyAppWeb.Presence.track(alice_socket, "user:#{alice.id}", %{online_at: 1})
    allow_pid(alice_socket.channel_pid)

    %{alice: alice, bob: bob, alice_socket: alice_socket, bob_socket: bob_socket}
  end

  test "alice sends, bob sees it persisted", %{alice_socket: a, bob_socket: b, bob: bob} do
    ref = push(a, "new_msg", %{"body" => "hi bob"})
    assert_reply ref, :ok

    # CI timing guard: wait for the broadcast to round-trip before DB read
    assert_push "new_msg", %{"body" => "hi bob"}

    # bob's channel pid also needs DB access on its side
    assert [%{body: "hi bob"}] = Chat.list_messages_for(bob.id)
    refute_receive _, 50
  end
end
```

## Common mistakes
- **Double allow** — calling `Sandbox.allow/3` twice on the same pid from the same parent raises `:already_shared`. Always gate through an idempotent helper that tracks granted pids.
- **Allowing Presence too late** — `Presence.track/3` hits the Repo synchronously in its `handle_call`; if you allow the tracker pid *after* the first track call, the tracker is already holding a broken connection. Allow it before the first track.
- **Forgetting the second channel** — the ChannelCase helper only fires on join. If you swap `socket.assigns` to impersonate a second user without a new `join/3`, that pid is never allowed.
- **Using `shared: true` in async tests** — mixing shared mode with `async: true` silently cross-contaminates tests. Pick one.
- **CI-only flake: `DBConnection.OwnershipError`** — locally the channel joins in <1ms, so the test's first `assert_receive` naturally waits. In CI, process scheduling differs and the parent races ahead of the channel's first DB call. Always use `assert_push` or `assert_receive` as a barrier **before** the first post-join Repo query, not `Process.sleep`.
- **Allowing from inside the channel** — `self()` inside `handle_in` is the channel pid, not the test pid. `allow/3` must be called from the test process that owns the sandbox checkout.
- **Multi-shard drift** — if you have `Repo.Primary` and `Repo.Replica`, each needs its own `allow/3` call per pid. One sandbox grant does not cover sibling Repos.

frontmatter.description: Uses start_owner!/2 for detached Ecto sandbox ownership that survives test pid death. Use when spawning LiveView/Task/GenServer children via start_supervised!. NOT for owned-by-test, NOT for async:false, NOT for Process.sleep hacks.
frontmatter.name: connection-ownership-transfer--survivor-pid

skill_genomes.skill_md_content:
---
name: connection-ownership-transfer--survivor-pid
description: Uses start_owner!/2 for detached Ecto sandbox ownership that survives test pid death. Use when spawning LiveView/Task/GenServer children via start_supervised!. NOT for owned-by-test, NOT for async:false, NOT for Process.sleep hacks.
---

# Survivor Pid: Ownership That Outlives the Test

## Quick Start
When your test pid dies, **the sandbox connection dies with it**. Any child process still holding that checkout will raise `DBConnection.OwnershipError` with a cryptic stack trace. The fix: use `Sandbox.start_owner!/2` to create a **detached owner** — a dedicated process whose only job is to hold the checkout. Children can `allow/3` against it and keep running after the test returns.

This is the correct pattern for any child that outlives the immediate test function: `start_supervised!`, `Task.Supervisor.async_nolink`, a LiveView mounted in `live_isolated`, or a GenServer started under a test supervisor.

## When to use
- A `start_supervised!` child queries the DB from its own callbacks
- A `Task.Supervisor` task may complete *after* the test pid exits
- A LiveView continues handling messages after your assertion block returns
- You see `DBConnection.OwnershipError` with `(client #PID<...> is still using a connection, but owner #PID<...> already exited)`
- The test is green but the log fills with ownership crashes during teardown

Do **not** use this when the test pid itself does all the DB work — plain `Sandbox.checkout/1` is simpler and correct there.

## Workflow
1. Call `Sandbox.start_owner!(Repo, shared: false)` at the **top** of the test. Store the returned pid.
2. Register `on_exit(fn -> Sandbox.stop_owner(owner) end)` immediately — before spawning anything. This is the only cleanup path that works; `ExUnit`'s default sandbox teardown will not find the detached owner.
3. Spawn children via `start_supervised!` / `Task.Supervisor.async_nolink` / `live_isolated` — whatever outlives the test pid.
4. For each child pid, call `Sandbox.allow(Repo, owner, child_pid)`. The `owner` argument is the detached owner from step 1 — **not** `self()`.
5. Run assertions. Children may keep executing after your `assert` returns; they remain allowed until `stop_owner` fires.
6. On test exit, `on_exit` runs, `stop_owner` checks the connection back in, children see a clean shutdown (not an ownership crash).

Ownership transfer is still synchronous — `allow/3` returns when the child is registered. The *survival* property comes from the owner pid, not from timing.

## Examples

### Example 1: Task.Supervisor that finishes after the test

```elixir
defmodule MyApp.LateTaskTest do
  use MyApp.DataCase, async: true

  alias Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox
  alias MyApp.{Repo, Accounts, Mailer}

  test "enqueued welcome email persists audit row even if task outlives test" do
    owner = Sandbox.start_owner!(Repo, shared: false)
    on_exit(fn -> Sandbox.stop_owner(owner) end)

    {:ok, user} = Accounts.create_user(%{email: "a@b.test"})

    {:ok, task_sup} = start_supervised(Task.Supervisor)

    task =
      Task.Supervisor.async_nolink(task_sup, fn ->
        Mailer.deliver_welcome_and_audit(user)
      end)

    Sandbox.allow(Repo, owner, task.pid)

    # We intentionally do NOT await the task — it may finish after this block.
    assert Accounts.get_user!(user.id).email == "a@b.test"
    # Test pid exits here. owner stays up until on_exit. task completes cleanly.
  end
end
```

### Example 2: LiveView with a handle_info that runs post-assertion

```elixir
defmodule MyAppWeb.DashboardLiveTest do
  use MyAppWeb.ConnCase, async: true
  import Phoenix.LiveViewTest

  alias Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox
  alias MyApp.Repo

  test "dashboard refreshes metrics after async broadcast", %{conn: conn} do
    owner = Sandbox.start_owner!(Repo, shared: false)
    on_exit(fn -> Sandbox.stop_owner(owner) end)

    {:ok, view, _html} = live_isolated(conn, MyAppWeb.DashboardLive)
    Sandbox.allow(Repo, owner, view.pid)

    send(view.pid, :refresh_metrics)
    assert render(view) =~ "Metrics loading"
    # handle_info/:refresh_metrics fires a DB query after we return;
    # owner survives, so the query succeeds instead of crashing teardown.
  end
end
```

## Common mistakes

- **Passing `self()` to `allow/3`** after using `start_owner!`. The whole point is that `self()` is about to die — you must pass the detached owner pid.
- **Forgetting `on_exit(stop_owner)`**. The detached owner leaks across tests and eventually exhausts the pool. ExUnit's built-in sandbox cleanup does not know about it.
- **Mixing `shared: true` with `start_owner!`**. Shared mode already pins ownership to the test pid; the two strategies fight each other. Pick one per test.
- **Calling `start_owner!` inside a `setup` that also checks out with `Sandbox.checkout/1`**. You end up with two owners and the child gets allowed against the wrong one. Use `start_owner!` *or* `checkout`, never both in the same test.
- **Reaching for `Process.sleep(50)` when you see the ownership crash.** Sleep does not keep a dead pid alive. The error means the owner already exited; no amount of waiting fixes that. Only a surviving owner does.
- **Allowing before `start_supervised!` returns.** You need the real child pid. Call `start_supervised!` first, then `allow`, then interact.

frontmatter.description: Bisect flaky Ecto sandbox tests to find interacting pair, then walk a hypothesis tree of ownership and global-state causes. Use when "passes alone, fails in suite". NOT for real logic bugs or single-test failures.
frontmatter.name: flaky-test-diagnosis--bisection-tree

skill_genomes.skill_md_content:
---
name: flaky-test-diagnosis--bisection-tree
description: Bisect flaky Ecto sandbox tests to find interacting pair, then walk a hypothesis tree of ownership and global-state causes. Use when "passes alone, fails in suite". NOT for real logic bugs or single-test failures.
---

# Bisection + Hypothesis Tree

## Quick Start
Flaky "passes alone, fails in suite" bugs are **interaction bugs**. You cannot fix what you cannot localize. First bisect to find the offending pair, then walk the hypothesis tree to name the cause, then eliminate the leaf. Never add `Process.sleep`.

## When to use
- A test passes with `mix test path/to/test.exs` but fails inside the full suite
- Failure is order-dependent (changes with `--seed`)
- You see `DBConnection.OwnershipError`, stale data, unique constraint violations on "fresh" rows, or a GenServer already started
- NOT for genuine logic bugs (fails alone too)
- NOT for single-test timeouts on slow queries
- NOT for CI-only environment failures (those are config, not ownership)

## Workflow

### Step 1 — Bisect to the interacting pair
```bash
# Confirm order-dependence and capture the bad seed
mix test --seed 424242 --trace | tee /tmp/fail.log

# Re-run with same seed to confirm it's deterministic (not wall-clock flaky)
mix test --seed 424242 --trace

# Bisect: run the failing test + a shrinking prefix until you find the minimum set
mix test --seed 424242 test/a_test.exs test/b_test.exs test/failing_test.exs
# Keep halving the prefix until removing any file makes the failure disappear.
```
The output is **a pair**: `(culprit_test, victim_test)`. Now you know where to look.

### Step 2 — Walk the hypothesis tree
```
flaky test (passes alone, fails in suite)
|
+-- A. Ownership leak (most common)
|   +-- A1. Spawned process does DB work without allow/3
|   |       -> Task.async / Task.Supervisor / spawn_link inside the test
|   +-- A2. Shared mode leaked from an earlier test
|   |       -> set_mode(:shared) without on_exit cleanup
|   +-- A3. Real GenServer owns the conn across tests
|           -> start_supervised used for a named singleton
|
+-- B. Global mutable state
|   +-- B1. Application.put_env not reverted in on_exit
|   +-- B2. :persistent_term / :ets table written and not cleared
|   +-- B3. Mox :global mode bleeding into async tests
|
+-- C. Name collisions
|   +-- C1. Process.register/Registry using a hard-coded name
|   +-- C2. start_supervised of a named GenServer across tests
|
+-- D. Non-ownership flakiness (still real, still yours to fix)
    +-- D1. Deterministic seed data + unique constraint
    |       -> two tests both insert user with email "a@b.com"
    +-- D2. Time-of-day: Date.utc_today/0, NaiveDateTime.utc_now/0
    |       -> fails only when the suite runs across midnight UTC
    +-- D3. Floating-point / ordering assumptions (no ORDER BY)
```

### Step 3 — Verify each candidate leaf
For the suspect leaf, instrument or refactor to **eliminate** it, not hide it.
- Spawned process? Grep the culprit for `Task.`, `spawn`, `GenServer.start`. Add `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.allow(Repo, parent, child)` at spawn time, or restructure so the DB call happens in the test process.
- Global state? Grep for `Application.put_env`, `:ets.`, `:persistent_term.`, `Mox.set_mox_global`. Add matching cleanup in `on_exit/1` or remove the global entirely.
- Name collision? Grep `Process.register`, `name: __MODULE__`. Use `start_supervised!` with a per-test unique name, or `Registry.register` with the test pid as the key.
- Seed collision? Add `System.unique_integer/1` to fixture factories.
- Time bug? Inject a clock — `Application.get_env(:app, :clock, DateTime)`.

### Step 4 — Prove the fix
Re-run the exact failing invocation from Step 1. It must pass. Then re-run `mix test --seed 0` **three times** with different seeds. Silence on all three = fixed.

## Examples

### Example 1 — Bisection locates an ownership leak in Task.async
```elixir
# BAD — culprit test (notifications_test.exs)
test "fans out notifications", %{user: user} do
  Enum.each(1..5, fn _ ->
    Task.async(fn ->
      # DIFFERENT PROCESS — does not own the sandbox checkout
      Repo.insert!(%Notification{user_id: user.id})
    end)
    |> Task.await()
  end)
end

# Bisection showed notifications_test.exs corrupts state seen by
# accounts_test.exs. Hypothesis tree leaf A1 (spawned process, no allow/3).

# GOOD — allow the child to borrow the parent's connection
test "fans out notifications", %{user: user} do
  parent = self()

  Enum.each(1..5, fn _ ->
    Task.async(fn ->
      Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.allow(Repo, parent, self())
      Repo.insert!(%Notification{user_id: user.id})
    end)
    |> Task.await()
  end)
end
```

### Example 2 — Non-ownership: deterministic seed data collides
```elixir
# BAD — every test inserts the same email; fails when two tests run in the
# same sandboxed transaction under shared mode, or when one forgets to sandbox.
def user_fixture(attrs \\ %{}) do
  Repo.insert!(%User{email: "user@example.com"} |> Map.merge(attrs))
end

# Bisection found (signup_test.exs, profile_test.exs). Tree leaf D1: unique
# constraint collision from deterministic seed data, not an ownership bug.

# GOOD — unique per call, still deterministic within one test
def user_fixture(attrs \\ %{}) do
  i = System.unique_integer([:positive])
  defaults = %{email: "user-#{i}@example.com", handle: "u#{i}"}
  Repo.insert!(struct(User, Map.merge(defaults, attrs)))
end
```

## Common mistakes
- **Adding `Process.sleep/1`** — hides the race, reappears on a slower machine.
- **Setting `async: false` on every test** — masks the bug and kills suite speed. Fix the ownership, keep `async: true`.
- **Blaming "flaky CI"** — reproduce locally with the bad seed. It is deterministic, you just have not localized it yet.
- **Fixing the victim instead of the culprit** — the failing test is the symptom. The preceding test in the bisected pair is where the leak lives.
- **Skipping Step 4's three-seed re-run** — one green run after a fix is not proof. Different seed, different evidence.
- **Assuming ownership** — walk the whole tree. Time-of-day bugs and seed collisions look identical at the error message level.

frontmatter.description: Maps every process LiveView spawns in tests and threads Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox metadata so render_async sees real DB state. Use when LiveView tests hit Ecto. NOT for dead Phoenix views or Wallaby browser suites.
frontmatter.name: liveview-sandbox-integration--process-tree-map

skill_genomes.skill_md_content:
---
name: liveview-sandbox-integration--process-tree-map
description: Maps every process LiveView spawns in tests and threads Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox metadata so render_async sees real DB state. Use when LiveView tests hit Ecto. NOT for dead Phoenix views or Wallaby browser suites.
---

# LiveView Process Tree Sandbox Integration

## Quick Start
A LiveView test does not run in one process. It runs in *at least* four:

1. The **ExUnit test process** (owns the sandbox checkout).
2. The **LiveView mount process** (`Phoenix.LiveView.Channel`, spawned by `live/2`).
3. Any **`assign_async` / `start_async` Task** processes (one per async assign).
4. Any **LiveComponent update** calls and **`handle_info`** messages (same LV process, but asserted against a snapshot after async resolution).

Every one of those processes needs sandbox ownership, or the first DB query raises `DBConnection.OwnershipError`. `Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox` solves this by stamping ownership metadata onto an HTTP header during Conn setup; the LiveView channel reads the header on mount and calls `Sandbox.allow/3` for itself and its children.

## When to use
Use when writing LiveView tests that touch Ecto — `mount/3` queries, `handle_event` writes, `assign_async` pipelines, `handle_info` refreshes, or LiveComponent lifecycle callbacks that query. Required for any test using `Phoenix.LiveViewTest.live/2` against a LiveView that is not purely presentational.

NOT for dead-view `Phoenix.ConnTest` tests (the plug alone is enough), non-LiveView channel tests, or Wallaby/Hound end-to-end browser suites — those have their own ownership models.

## Workflow
1. **Add the plug to `endpoint.ex`** inside `if code_reloading? do` (or behind `Mix.env() == :test`). Place it *before* `Plug.Session` and the router so it runs on every request.
2. **Wire `DataCase` / `ConnCase`** to call `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.checkout/2` in `setup` and set `:shared` mode only when strictly needed (prefer per-test ownership).
3. **Stamp the conn** in `ConnCase` with `Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox.metadata_for(Repo, self())` and inject it via `Plug.Conn.put_req_header/3` on `"x-phoenix-ecto-sandbox"` (or use `Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox.metadata_header/2`).
4. **Call `live/2` with the stamped conn** — the channel process now inherits ownership automatically; you do NOT need to call `Sandbox.allow/3` by hand.
5. **Render async assigns** with `render_async(view, timeout)` before asserting. This blocks until every in-flight `assign_async` Task completes. Never `Process.sleep/1` — it races with Task scheduling and produces flakes that only fail in CI.
6. **Assert after resolution** using `render(view)` or `has_element?/3`. If you need intermediate loading states, assert them *before* `render_async`.

## Examples

### Example 1 — `endpoint.ex` plug wiring
```elixir
defmodule MyAppWeb.Endpoint do
  use Phoenix.Endpoint, otp_app: :my_app

  # Must run before the router so ownership is set before any query.
  if Application.compile_env(:my_app, :sql_sandbox, false) do
    plug Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox,
      at: "/sandbox",
      header: "x-phoenix-ecto-sandbox",
      repo: MyApp.Repo
  end

  plug Plug.Session, @session_options
  plug MyAppWeb.Router
end

# config/test.exs
config :my_app, sql_sandbox: true
config :my_app, MyApp.Repo, pool: Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox
```

### Example 2 — LiveView test with `assign_async` and `render_async`
```elixir
defmodule MyAppWeb.DashboardLiveTest do
  use MyAppWeb.ConnCase, async: true
  import Phoenix.LiveViewTest
  alias MyApp.Repo

  setup do
    :ok = Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.checkout(Repo)
    # Stamp the conn so LiveView channel inherits ownership.
    metadata = Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox.metadata_for(Repo, self())
    conn =
      Phoenix.ConnTest.build_conn()
      |> Plug.Conn.put_req_header("x-phoenix-ecto-sandbox", metadata)

    user = insert!(:user, name: "Ada")
    insert!(:booking, user_id: user.id, status: "confirmed")
    {:ok, conn: conn, user: user}
  end

  test "dashboard resolves assign_async bookings", %{conn: conn, user: user} do
    {:ok, view, html} = live(conn, ~p"/dashboard/#{user.id}")

    # Loading state renders synchronously on mount.
    assert html =~ "Loading bookings"

    # Block until every assign_async Task in the LV process tree resolves.
    html = render_async(view, 500)

    assert html =~ "confirmed"
    assert has_element?(view, "[data-role=booking-row]", "Ada")
  end
end
```

## Common mistakes
- **Forgetting the plug in `endpoint.ex`.** Symptom: test passes `live/2`, but the first query inside `mount/3` raises `DBConnection.OwnershipError: cannot find ownership process for #PID<...>`. The conn header is set, but there is no plug to read it. Fix: add `plug Phoenix.Ecto.SQL.Sandbox` before the router.
- **Using `Process.sleep(100)` instead of `render_async/2`.** The `assign_async` Task is scheduled on a different process; sleep is a guess, not a sync point. On a loaded CI box the sleep expires before the Task finishes and you get an empty render. Always `render_async`.
- **Calling `Sandbox.allow/3` manually on the LV pid.** Unnecessary and racy — the plug already did it during channel join. Manual `allow/3` on a pid that already owns the connection raises `already allowed`.
- **Setting `Sandbox.mode(:shared)` by default.** Shared mode serializes the whole suite and hides ownership bugs. Use per-test ownership and let the plug thread metadata through the process tree.
- **Asserting before `render_async`.** The loading-state HTML is what `live/2` returns. If you assert on final data without awaiting async, you are asserting against the skeleton.
- **Not enabling `sql_sandbox` in `config/test.exs`.** The compile-time guard around the plug means the plug is absent in test unless the flag is set. Symptom looks identical to "forgot the plug."

frontmatter.description: Oban testing modes as switchboard — :inline, :manual (default), :shared — with perform_job/3 and with_testing_mode/2 for sandbox tests. Use when picking a mode or switching mid-test. NOT for production config.
frontmatter.name: oban-sandbox-integration--testing-mode-switchboard

skill_genomes.skill_md_content:
---
name: oban-sandbox-integration--testing-mode-switchboard
description: Oban testing modes as switchboard — :inline, :manual (default), :shared — with perform_job/3 and with_testing_mode/2 for sandbox tests. Use when picking a mode or switching mid-test. NOT for production config.
---

# Oban Testing Mode Switchboard

## Quick Start
Oban has three testing modes. `:manual` is the default best choice: jobs are inserted but never executed, so you invoke them directly with `perform_job/3` inside the test process — which is the only process that can see uncommitted sandbox data. `drain_queue/1` looks like it should work, but it doesn't: the queue runs in a separate process that cannot see your checked-out sandbox connection, so it sees zero jobs and executes nothing.

## When to use
Use when you're writing tests for Oban workers that touch `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox`, when picking between `:inline`, `:manual`, and `:shared`, when you need to flip modes mid-test with `Oban.Testing.with_testing_mode/2`, or when `drain_queue/1` silently passes zero jobs and you can't figure out why.

## The Three Modes

| Mode | Where jobs run | Sees sandbox data? | `async: true` safe? | Use when |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `:inline` | Inserter's process (synchronously on `insert`) | Yes | Yes | You want insertion itself to trigger execution; tolerate zero queueing semantics |
| `:manual` **(default)** | Nowhere until you call `perform_job/3` | Yes (inside test process) | Yes | 95% of unit tests — direct, deterministic, sandbox-safe |
| `:shared` | Real Oban queue processes | Only with shared sandbox ownership | **No, `async: false`** | True end-to-end integration with the real queue |

Set the default in `config/test.exs`:
```elixir
config :my_app, Oban, testing: :manual
```

## Workflow
1. In `config/test.exs`, set `testing: :manual` on your Oban config — this is the default for nearly every test.
2. In the test module, add `use Oban.Testing, repo: MyApp.Repo` so `perform_job/3` and `assert_enqueued/1` are in scope.
3. For unit tests of worker logic, call `perform_job(MyWorker, %{"key" => value}, attempt: 1)` directly. String-keyed args — workers receive `%Oban.Job{args: ...}` deserialized from JSONB.
4. For tests that assert a caller enqueued a job (without running it), use `assert_enqueued(worker: MyWorker, args: %{...})` and then optionally `perform_job/3` the result.
5. If a single test needs `:inline` (e.g. you're testing the "insert triggers side effect" flow), wrap just that block in `Oban.Testing.with_testing_mode(:inline, fn -> ... end)`.
6. If a test truly needs `:shared`, mark it `async: false`, use `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.mode(Repo, {:shared, self()})`, and accept that it's an integration test, not a unit test.
7. **Never call `Oban.drain_queue/1` in sandbox tests.** The queue runs in a separate BEAM process that does not own your sandbox checkout; it sees an empty `oban_jobs` table and drains zero jobs. The test passes for the wrong reason.

## Examples

### Example 1 — `:manual` mode with `perform_job/3` (the 95% case)
```elixir
defmodule MyApp.Workers.WelcomeEmailWorkerTest do
  use MyApp.DataCase, async: true
  use Oban.Testing, repo: MyApp.Repo

  alias MyApp.Workers.WelcomeEmailWorker

  test "sends welcome email for a freshly inserted user" do
    # Sandbox data — only visible inside this test process
    user = insert(:user, email: "ada@example.com", welcomed_at: nil)

    # perform_job runs the worker INLINE in the test process,
    # so it sees `user` even though the transaction hasn't committed.
    assert :ok = perform_job(WelcomeEmailWorker, %{"user_id" => user.id})

    assert Repo.reload!(user).welcomed_at != nil
  end

  test "caller enqueues the job with the right args" do
    user = insert(:user)
    {:ok, _} = MyApp.Accounts.welcome(user)

    # Assert the job was queued without executing it.
    assert_enqueued(
      worker: WelcomeEmailWorker,
      args: %{"user_id" => user.id}
    )
  end
end
```

### Example 2 — `with_testing_mode/2` for an `:inline` block, plus the `drain_queue` trap
```elixir
defmodule MyApp.BillingFlowTest do
  use MyApp.DataCase, async: true
  use Oban.Testing, repo: MyApp.Repo

  test "charging an invoice immediately runs the receipt worker (:inline)" do
    invoice = insert(:invoice, state: "pending")

    # For this one block, flip to :inline so `Billing.charge/1`'s
    # internal `Oban.insert!/1` executes the ReceiptWorker synchronously.
    Oban.Testing.with_testing_mode(:inline, fn ->
      assert {:ok, _} = MyApp.Billing.charge(invoice)
    end)

    assert Repo.reload!(invoice).state == "paid"
  end

  test "DO NOT do this — drain_queue sees zero jobs" do
    user = insert(:user)
    {:ok, _} = MyApp.Accounts.welcome(user)

    # ANTI-PATTERN. Oban.drain_queue/1 runs queue workers in a
    # separate process that doesn't own the sandbox checkout,
    # so it sees an empty oban_jobs table. This assertion passes
    # for the wrong reason — zero jobs drained, zero failures.
    #
    # %{success: 0, failure: 0} = Oban.drain_queue(queue: :default)
    #
    # Correct: use perform_job/3 or assert_enqueued/1 in :manual mode.
    assert_enqueued(worker: MyApp.Workers.WelcomeEmailWorker)
  end
end
```

## Common mistakes
- **Calling `Oban.drain_queue/1` in a sandbox test.** The drain runs in a different process; it cannot see your uncommitted sandbox data. You get `%{success: 0, failure: 0}` and a green test that asserts nothing. Use `perform_job/3` instead.
- **Atom-keyed args to `perform_job/3`.** Oban serializes args to JSONB, so workers always receive string keys. Pass `%{"user_id" => id}`, not `%{user_id: id}`, or your worker's pattern match fails.
- **Using `:shared` mode with `async: true`.** `:shared` requires a single sandbox owner; concurrent tests will clobber each other's data. Either stay in `:manual` or mark the test `async: false` with `Sandbox.mode(Repo, {:shared, self()})`.
- **Setting `testing: :inline` globally.** Inline mode executes on insert, which breaks tests that want to assert "this job was enqueued" separately from "this job ran." Default to `:manual` and opt into `:inline` per-block with `with_testing_mode/2`.
- **Forgetting `use Oban.Testing, repo: MyApp.Repo`.** Without it, `perform_job/3`, `assert_enqueued/1`, and `with_testing_mode/2` aren't imported and you'll see cryptic `UndefinedFunctionError`s.
- **Asserting on `Oban.Job` return values from `perform_job/3`.** It returns whatever the worker's `perform/1` returned (`:ok`, `{:ok, term}`, `{:error, term}`, `{:snooze, n}`, `{:cancel, reason}`). Match on the worker's contract, not on an `%Oban.Job{}` struct.

frontmatter.description: Decide sandbox checkout + mode via a flowchart. Use when setting up DataCase, choosing :manual vs :shared, or picking start_owner!/2 vs checkout/2. NOT for production pools or Repo.checkout/2.
frontmatter.name: sandbox-checkout-and-modes--decision-tree

skill_genomes.skill_md_content:
---
name: sandbox-checkout-and-modes--decision-tree
description: Decide sandbox checkout + mode via a flowchart. Use when setting up DataCase, choosing :manual vs :shared, or picking start_owner!/2 vs checkout/2. NOT for production pools or Repo.checkout/2.
---

# Sandbox Mode Decision Tree

## Quick Start
Mode selection is not a preference — it's a decision tree. Walk these four questions in order and the answer falls out:

1. Is this test code? If no, stop. Use your production pool config.
2. Does the test spawn processes that run queries? If no, `:manual` + `start_owner!` with defaults.
3. Do those processes outlive the test pid (unlinked `Task`, `GenServer.start`, `Oban.Testing`)? If yes, `start_owner!/2` is mandatory — `checkout/2` will leak.
4. Can you grant `allow/3` from the owner before the child queries? If yes, stay `:manual`. If no (fire-and-forget children, dynamic pids), use `shared: true`.

## When to use
Use this variant when configuring `MyApp.DataCase`, debugging `DBConnection.OwnershipError`, or reviewing a PR that touches sandbox setup. Do NOT use it for `Repo.transaction/2` isolation, production connection pooling, or non-Ecto test isolation.

## Mode comparison

| Mode | Owner lifetime | Allowed callers | When to use | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `:manual` | Bound to test pid via `start_owner!` | Explicit via `allow/3` | Default. Async-safe. | Must grant every child pid. |
| `{:shared, self()}` | Until test exits or mode reset | Every pid in the BEAM | Sync tests spawning dynamic children. Mid-test escape. | Serializes the whole suite. |
| `:shared` | Process-global | Every pid | Legacy sync-only suites. | Forces `async: false` everywhere. |
| `:auto` | Auto-checkout per query | N/A | Production pool config only. | NEVER in test code. |

## Workflow
1. Open `test/support/data_case.ex`. In `setup tags do`, compute `async? = Map.get(tags, :async, false)` — do not read `tags[:async]` in a guard, the key may be absent.
2. Call `pid = Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.start_owner!(MyApp.Repo, shared: not async?)`. This is the modern API — prefer it over `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.checkout/2`, which does not survive unlinked child processes.
3. Register teardown with `on_exit(fn -> Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.stop_owner(pid) end)`. `stop_owner/1` is idempotent; `checkin/2` is not — another reason to avoid the legacy pair.
4. If the test spawns a child that runs queries and you hold the pid, call `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.allow(MyApp.Repo, self(), child_pid)` BEFORE the child issues its first query. Ordering matters: `allow/3` after the query raises `OwnershipError`.
5. If the child pid is unknowable (e.g., an Oban worker dispatched mid-test, a `Phoenix.PubSub` subscriber, a `Task.Supervisor.start_child` without a monitor), escape upward: `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.mode(MyApp.Repo, {:shared, self()})`. Tag the test `@tag :async_unsafe` and set `use MyApp.DataCase, async: false` — `{:shared, self()}` and `async: true` are incompatible.
6. Never call `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.mode(Repo, :auto)` from a test. `:auto` is the production pool's mode. In tests it disables isolation entirely and every test starts seeing every other test's writes.
7. If a legacy file still calls `Sandbox.checkout(Repo)` followed by `Sandbox.mode(Repo, {:shared, self()})`, rewrite it as a single `start_owner!(Repo, shared: true)`. The old two-call form is the #1 source of "works locally, flakes in CI" reports.

## Examples

```elixir
# test/support/data_case.ex — decision tree realized
defmodule MyApp.DataCase do
  use ExUnit.CaseTemplate

  using do
    quote do
      alias MyApp.Repo
      import Ecto
      import Ecto.Changeset
      import Ecto.Query
      import MyApp.DataCase
    end
  end

  setup tags do
    async? = Map.get(tags, :async, false)

    # Q1: test code? yes. Q2: spawns procs? unknown at template level.
    # Default to :manual (shared: not async?) and let individual tests escalate.
    pid = Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.start_owner!(MyApp.Repo, shared: not async?)
    on_exit(fn -> Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.stop_owner(pid) end)

    :ok
  end
end
```

```elixir
# test/my_app/order_dispatcher_test.exs — mid-test escape hatch
defmodule MyApp.OrderDispatcherTest do
  # async: false is REQUIRED with {:shared, self()} — the decision tree
  # says "dynamic unknowable pids => shared". Pay the serialization cost.
  use MyApp.DataCase, async: false

  test "dispatches to dynamically spawned workers" do
    # Q4: can we grant allow/3 before the child queries? No — the supervisor
    # spawns workers on demand and we never see the pids. Escalate.
    Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.mode(MyApp.Repo, {:shared, self()})

    {:ok, order} = MyApp.Orders.create(%{items: [1, 2, 3]})
    MyApp.OrderDispatcher.dispatch_async(order.id)

    # Child Task runs under the shared owner; no allow/3 needed.
    assert_eventually(fn -> MyApp.Orders.get!(order.id).status == :dispatched end)
  end
end
```

## Common mistakes
- Calling `Sandbox.mode(Repo, :auto)` in a test to "fix" an ownership error. `:auto` is production-only; it masks the bug by removing isolation.
- Using `Sandbox.checkout/2` when children outlive the test pid — the legacy API does not transfer ownership to unlinked processes, so the child sees `OwnershipError` and the test passes anyway because the assertion ran on the owner's (stale) view.
- Granting `allow/3` AFTER the child has already queried. The grant must precede the first query on that connection.
- Pairing `async: true` with `{:shared, self()}`. The shared mode serializes the pool; any concurrent test will block or fail. Set `async: false` on any file that escalates to shared.
- Flipping `async: false` as a remedy for flakes without diagnosing ownership. Async-false hides the symptom; the owner-allowlist mismatch is still there and will re-emerge the moment a sibling test spawns a background process.
- Calling `start_owner!/2` twice in the same test (e.g., once in the template's `setup` and again in a test-local `setup`). The second call raises; use `allow/3` from the first owner instead.

frontmatter.description: Frames Ecto shared mode as a deliberate trade with a cost ledger. Use when a test truly needs cross-process visibility and all cheaper alternatives fail. NOT for convenience, default use, or silencing OwnershipError.
frontmatter.name: shared-mode-fallback--cost-ledger

skill_genomes.skill_md_content:
---
name: shared-mode-fallback--cost-ledger
description: Frames Ecto shared mode as a deliberate trade with a cost ledger. Use when a test truly needs cross-process visibility and all cheaper alternatives fail. NOT for convenience, default use, or silencing OwnershipError.
---

# Shared Mode — Cost-of-Shared-Mode Ledger

## Quick Start
Shared mode is not free. Every call to `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.mode(Repo, {:shared, self()})` pays a bill. Write down what you're buying, what it costs, and prove no cheaper option exists. If you cannot name a concrete gain, do not sign the ledger.

## When to use

Use this variant **when you have hit an `OwnershipError`** (or are about to) and you are tempted to reach for `{:shared, self()}` as the fix. Before you do, walk through the ledger: every cost must be offset by a **named, concrete gain**. If you cannot articulate the gain in one sentence, you are not ready for shared mode — a cheaper alternative still exists.

**NOT for**: making `async: true` "just work" again, suppressing errors without understanding them, or applying as a module-wide default because one test case needs cross-process visibility.

## The Cost Ledger

Every time you enable shared mode, you incur three costs. Write them down in a comment next to the `setup` block.

| Cost | What it means | Who pays |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Loss of `async: true` | The entire module must run `async: false`. Your test suite wall-clock grows. | CI, every future run |
| 2. Pollution risk | Any side effect (ETS, GenServer state, process dict) from another test bleeds in because the sandbox no longer isolates processes. | Flaky-test debugger, six months from now |
| 3. Debugging cost | A future engineer will ask "why is this module the slowest in the suite?" and will have to re-derive your reasoning. | The person who inherits the file |

Each cost must be justified by a **named gain**. Legitimate gains include:

- We must run a real **Oban queue worker** that checks out its own connection
- A **C-port / NIF** spawns its own OS process and cannot inherit the caller's allowance
- A **third-party library** (e.g., `Cachex`, `GenStage`, `Flow`) owns its own supervision tree and connection checkout cannot be threaded through
- We are testing **LiveView reconnection** across a genuinely new process and `allow/3` cannot reach it

"It was easier" is not a gain.

## Workflow — Ordered Alternatives

Check each alternative **in order**. Only reach for `{:shared, self()}` when every one has been ruled out with a reason.

1. **`Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.allow(Repo, parent_pid, allowed_pid)`** — The cheapest fix. If you know the PID of the process that needs DB access (Task, GenServer, Agent), explicitly allow it. Preserves `async: true`. Works for 80% of cases.
2. **`start_supervised/2` with the test owner as caller** — Starting a supervised process under ExUnit means its lifetime is scoped to the test, and you can pass `caller: self()` so checkouts flow through the owner.
3. **Telemetry events instead of polling** — If you're tempted to spin up a worker just to observe its effect, attach a telemetry handler in your test process and assert on the event. The worker never needs its own connection.
4. **Inline / synchronous testing mode** — Libraries like Oban (`testing: :inline`) and Broadway (`producer: [module: {Broadway.DummyProducer, []}]`) offer inline modes that execute in the calling process. No cross-process checkout needed.
5. **Refactor the code under test** — Sometimes the honest answer is that the production code spawns a process where it didn't need to. A GenServer that could be a function doesn't need its own connection.
6. **Only now**: `Sandbox.mode(Repo, {:shared, self()})`. Mark `async: false`. Write the ledger comment. Name the gain.

## Examples

### Example 1 — Walking the ledger (correct)

```elixir
defmodule BoothIQ.Workers.ScanProcessorTest do
  # LEDGER: We run a real Oban queue worker here because we are testing the
  # Oban telemetry -> DB write path end-to-end. Alternatives ruled out:
  #   - allow/3: Oban worker PID is not known at setup time
  #   - testing: :inline: we specifically need the queue scheduler behavior
  #   - telemetry-only: we assert on a DB row written by the worker
  # COST ACCEPTED: async: false (this module only), ~200ms slower in CI.
  use BoothIQ.DataCase, async: false

  setup do
    :ok = Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.checkout(BoothIQ.Repo)
    Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.mode(BoothIQ.Repo, {:shared, self()})
  end

  test "worker writes scan result to DB" do
    %{id: scan_id} = insert(:scan)
    {:ok, _job} = Oban.insert(BoothIQ.Workers.ScanProcessor.new(%{scan_id: scan_id}))
    assert_receive {:scan_processed, ^scan_id}, 1_000
    assert Repo.get(BoothIQ.Scan, scan_id).status == "processed"
  end
end
```

### Example 2 — The cheaper alternative won (correct)

```elixir
defmodule BoothIQ.Reports.GeneratorTest do
  # Previously used shared mode. Replaced with allow/3 after noticing the
  # spawned Task's PID was available at the call site. async: true restored.
  use BoothIQ.DataCase, async: true

  test "report generator writes rows from a spawned Task" do
    parent = self()
    task =
      Task.async(fn ->
        receive do
          :go -> BoothIQ.Reports.Generator.run(parent)
        end
      end)

    :ok = Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.allow(BoothIQ.Repo, self(), task.pid)
    send(task.pid, :go)
    assert Task.await(task) == :ok
    assert Repo.aggregate(BoothIQ.Report, :count) == 1
  end
end
```

## Common mistakes

- **Reaching for shared mode on the first `OwnershipError`**. The error is information: some process needs access that it doesn't have. Find *which* process and `allow/3` it before escalating.
- **Applying shared mode module-wide for one test case**. If only one test needs it, move that test to its own `async: false` module. Don't tax every other test in the file.
- **Forgetting to mark `async: false`**. Shared mode with `async: true` is undefined behavior — your tests will race against each other for the single shared connection.
- **No ledger comment**. Six months from now, someone will ask "why is this module async: false?" and will delete the shared-mode call without understanding it. Leave a paper trail.
- **Treating shared mode as equivalent to allow/3**. `allow/3` is surgical — it grants one PID access. Shared mode is a hammer — every process inherits access. Use the smallest tool that works.
- **Using shared mode to paper over production code that spawns processes gratuitously**. If a module spawns a Task just to wrap a synchronous call, the fix is in the production code, not the test.

# Connection Ownership State Machine

## Quick Start
A sandboxed Ecto transaction is owned by exactly one pid at a time. Every test isolation bug is a question about ownership: *who owns this connection right now, and is the process doing the query on that owner's allow-list?* Start there before touching case templates.

## When to use
- A spawned `Task`, `GenServer`, `Agent`, or Oban worker raises `DBConnection.OwnershipError`
- Background process can't see data the test just inserted
- Tests pass on first run, fail on retry, or vice versa
- Reviewing code that uses `Task.async`, `Task.Supervisor.async_nolink`, `spawn`, or `Process.send_after` inside a test
- Debugging LiveView tests where the mounted process can't read fixtures

## Workflow
1. **Identify the owner pid.** In a test, that's `self()`. The sandbox transaction belongs to this pid.
2. **Trace every query back to a pid.** For each `Repo.*` call in the code under test, ask: which process executes this query? If it's not `self()`, ownership matters.
3. **Enumerate the spawned processes.** List every process the test creates: `Task.async`, `start_supervised!`, LiveView mounts, Oban jobs, Phoenix Channels, GenServers under test.
4. **Grant allow/3 BEFORE the child queries.** Call `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.allow(MyApp.Repo, self(), child_pid)` after you know `child_pid` but before the child touches the Repo. Race here = flaky test.
5. **For dynamically spawned processes, use `:shared` mode as an escape hatch.** `Sandbox.mode(MyApp.Repo, {:shared, self()})` lets any process on the node see the owner's transaction. This forces `async: false` on that test — accept the cost deliberately, don't drift into it.
6. **Never transition to `:auto` in test code.** `:auto` commits outside a transaction and poisons the database for every other test.

## Examples

```elixir
# Task spawned inside a test — explicit ownership transfer
test "background enrichment writes to the repo", %{user: user} do
  parent = self()

  task =
    Task.async(fn ->
      # This process is NOT the owner. It needs explicit permission.
      receive do
        :go -> Enrichment.run(user)
      end
    end)

  :ok = Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.allow(MyApp.Repo, parent, task.pid)
  send(task.pid, :go)
  Task.await(task)

  assert Repo.get!(User, user.id).enriched_at
end
```

```elixir
# Dynamically supervised children (Oban, Registry-spawned workers) —
# shared mode is the honest choice. async: false is not a workaround,
# it's the contract.
defmodule MyApp.ObanCase do
  use ExUnit.CaseTemplate

  using do
    quote do
      import Ecto.Query
      alias MyApp.Repo
    end
  end

  setup tags do
    pid = Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.start_owner!(MyApp.Repo, shared: not tags[:async])

    if tags[:shared_sandbox] do
      Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.mode(MyApp.Repo, {:shared, self()})
    end

    on_exit(fn -> Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.stop_owner(pid) end)
    :ok
  end
end
```

## Common mistakes
- Calling `Sandbox.allow/3` AFTER the child process has already queried — the child raced ahead and saw no connection.
- Using `async: false` as a shotgun fix for `OwnershipError` without naming the spawned process. If you can't point at the pid, you haven't fixed the bug.
- Granting allow/3 from inside the child process (`Sandbox.allow(Repo, parent, self())`) — ownership must be transferred by a process that already holds it.
- Forgetting that Oban's `Oban.Testing` helpers run jobs inline by default — this IS the test pid, so allow/3 isn't needed there, but `perform_job/3` in a separate process IS.
- Assuming `{:shared, self()}` mode is reversible mid-test. It is not; it pins the whole Repo for the duration.
- Confusing the Tidewave MCP dev-DB connection with the test sandbox — if you see data in `iex -S mix` that your tests can't see, you're querying the wrong database entirely.

---

# Tidewave MCP Audit Protocol

## Quick Start

When an AI agent debugs a failing Ecto test with access to a dev-DB MCP tool (Tidewave, Postgres MCP, etc.), there is a systemic risk: **the MCP tool is wired to the dev Repo, but the test failure happened inside a sandboxed test Repo**. Reading the dev DB tells you nothing about the test. Worse, it often looks plausible — empty tables, stale rows, missing records — and the agent starts "fixing" the wrong database.

This variant defines a three-step audit protocol that MUST run before any MCP query during test debugging.

From the BoothIQ post-mortem:
> "Claude uses Tidewave's dev DB connection and thinks it's looking at the test DB — which is always empty."

That quote describes a real, observed failure. This protocol exists so it does not happen again.

## When to use

Use when ALL of the following are true:
- You are debugging a failing `mix test` / ExUnit run
- The test hits `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox`
- A dev-DB MCP tool (Tidewave, Postgres MCP, pgcli MCP) is available in the session
- You are tempted to "just peek at the DB"

Do NOT use this protocol for:
- Production incident triage (dev MCP is also wrong there, but for different reasons)
- Non-Ecto test failures (HTTP, JSON, pure-logic)
- Dev-mode exploration where no test is involved

## Workflow

1. **Classify the failure.** Before touching the MCP tool, answer out loud: "Is the failing process a test process?" If the stacktrace contains `ExUnit`, `Test`, or the file is `*_test.exs`, the answer is YES.
2. **If YES: declare the MCP tool irrelevant.** Explicitly state: "Tidewave points at the dev Repo. The failing test owns a sandboxed test Repo. Any row count, any record, any schema state Tidewave reports is IRRELEVANT to this bug."
3. **Route the query through the test process.** Add a temporary `IO.inspect(Repo.all(Schema), label: "test-repo")` inside the failing test, or use `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.allow/3` plus a helper task. Re-run the test. Trust only that output.
4. **If you still reach for the MCP tool,** write a one-line justification: "I am querying the dev DB because ___." If the blank cannot be filled with a non-test reason, abort the query.
5. **Log the audit.** Leave a comment in the test: `# [mcp-audit] dev-DB MCP intentionally NOT consulted; test Repo is the only source of truth here.`

## Examples

### Example 1: Claude catches itself mid-debug

```text
User: The test `UserTest.test creates user with profile` is failing.
      The error is: "expected 1 user, got 0". Can you figure out why?

Claude: Let me check the database first.
        [about to call tidewave.query("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users")]

        Wait — MCP Audit Protocol, step 1.
        Is the failing process a test process? YES (UserTest, in *_test.exs).

        Step 2: Tidewave is wired to the dev Repo. The test owns a
        SANDBOXED connection. Whatever Tidewave tells me about the
        users table is about the DEV database, which has nothing to
        do with this test. It is IRRELEVANT.

        Step 3: I need to inspect the TEST Repo from inside the test
        process. Let me add an IO.inspect before the assertion.

Claude: (edits test)
        ```elixir
        test "creates user with profile" do
          {:ok, user} = Accounts.create_user_with_profile(%{name: "X"})
          IO.inspect(Repo.all(User), label: "test-repo users")
          assert length(Repo.all(User)) == 1
        end
        ```

        Re-running... output: `test-repo users: []`

        So the test Repo really is empty. That means
        `create_user_with_profile` either rolled back or never
        inserted. That's the real bug — nothing to do with Tidewave.
```

If Claude had skipped the audit and run `tidewave.query("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users")`, the dev DB would have returned `47` (leftover fixture data from manual testing). Claude would have concluded "users exist, the test is wrong" and chased a ghost for 20 minutes.

### Example 2: Justification fails, query aborted

```text
Claude: The test `OrderTest.test reserves inventory` fails with
        `** (MatchError) no match of right hand side value: nil`.

        Step 1: Is this a test process? YES.
        Step 2: Tidewave is IRRELEVANT to a sandboxed test.
        Step 4: Can I fill in "I am querying the dev DB because ___"?
                "…because I want to see the inventory table schema."
                But I can get that from the migration file or from
                `Repo.query!("\\d inventory")` routed through the test
                Repo. The dev DB is not the only source.
                Aborting MCP query.

        I will read the migration file instead.
```

## Common mistakes

- Consulting Tidewave "just to sanity-check" the DB is up. It is up. That is never the bug in a sandboxed test. You are rationalizing.
- Running an MCP query, seeing an empty table, and concluding "the test isn't inserting anything." The test Repo is a DIFFERENT empty table. Dev empty + test empty is a coincidence, not a signal.
- Using Tidewave to seed the dev DB so "Claude can see the fixtures." Tests do not read from the dev DB. Ever. This is the exact anti-pattern the post-mortem flags.
- Skipping step 1 because "it's obviously a test." The explicit classification step is what breaks the autopilot. Do not skip it.
- Forgetting the audit comment. Without the `[mcp-audit]` marker, the next debugging session will re-run the same flawed query.
